Benzenediazonium chloride reacts with fluoroboric acid ($\text{HBF}_4$) to yield benzenediazonium fluoroborate (Compound B).

$\text{Ar}-\text{N}_2^+\text{Cl}^- \xrightarrow{\text{HBF}_4} \text{Ar}-\text{N}_2^+\text{BF}_4^-$ (B)

When benzenediazonium fluoroborate is heated with aqueous $\text{NaNO}_2$ in the presence of copper, the diazonium group is replaced by a nitro group, forming nitrobenzene (Compound C).

$\text{Ar}-\text{N}_2^+\text{BF}_4^- \xrightarrow{\text{NaNO}_2, \text{Cu}, \Delta} \text{Ar}-\text{NO}_2$ (C)

Thus, Compound C is $\text{Ar}-\text{NO}_2$.

Answer: $\text{Ar} - \text{NO}_2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the reaction that converts $\text{CH}_3\text{CN}$ to $\text{CH}_3\text{-CH}_2\text{-NH}_2$ .
questions.solution:
The conversion of an alkyl cyanide (nitrile) to a primary amine by reduction is known as the Mendius reduction.

$\text{CH}_3\text{CN} + 4[\text{H}] \xrightarrow{\text{Na/C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}} \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_2$

Hoffmann reduction is used for converting amides to primary amines with one less carbon atom.

Clemmensen reduction is used for reducing aldehydes and ketones to alkanes.

Rosenmund reduction is used for reducing acid chlorides to aldehydes.

Answer: Mendius reduction

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the product when ethyl amine reacts with nitrous acid in cold.
questions.solution:
Ethyl amine ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_2$) is a primary aliphatic amine.

When a primary aliphatic amine reacts with nitrous acid ($\text{HNO}_2$) in cold conditions, it forms an unstable alkyl diazonium salt.

This diazonium salt readily decomposes to release nitrogen gas and forms a carbocation, which reacts with water present in the mixture to give ethyl alcohol ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$).

$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_2 + \text{HNO}_2 \rightarrow [\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{N}_2^{+}\text{Cl}^{-}] \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{N}_2 \uparrow + \text{HCl}$

Answer: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is least basic than aniline?
questions.solution:
The basicity of amines depends on the availability of the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom for protonation.

In aniline, the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom is delocalized over the benzene ring through resonance, making it less basic than aliphatic amines like $\text{CH}_3\text{NH}_2$ and ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$), where the lone pair is localized.

In N-methyl aniline, the electron-donating methyl group exerts a +I effect, which increases the electron density on the nitrogen atom, making it more basic than aniline.

In p-nitroaniline, the $-\text{NO}_2$ group is strongly electron-withdrawing due to its -I and -R effects. This significantly decreases the electron density on the nitrogen atom, making the lone pair even less available for protonation compared to aniline.

Therefore, p-nitroaniline is less basic than aniline.

Answer: p-nitroaniline

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What type of sugar is the stachyose?
questions.solution:
Stachyose is an oligosaccharide that yields four monosaccharide molecules upon hydrolysis. It consists of two galactose units, one glucose unit, and one fructose unit.

Since it produces four monosaccharide units on hydrolysis, it is classified as a tetrasaccharide.

Answer: tetrasaccharide

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the number of stereo structure of glucose?
questions.solution:
The open-chain structure of glucose is an aldohexose with the chemical formula $CHO-(CHOH)_4-CH_2OH$.

In this structure, there are $4$ chiral carbon atoms (the four $CHOH$ groups).

Since the molecule is unsymmetrical (the two terminal groups are different, $CHO$ and $CH_2OH$), the total number of stereoisomers is given by the formula $2^n$, where $n$ is the number of chiral centers.

Substituting $n = 4$, we get $2^4 = 16$.

Thus, there are $16$ possible stereo structures for an aldohexose like glucose.

Answer: $16$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is having largest size?
questions.solution:
The size of an antibody (a protein molecule) is approximately $10$ nm.

The size of a typical virus ranges from $20$ nm to $300$ nm.

The size of a typical bacterium ranges from $0.5$ $\mu$m to $5$ $\mu$m ($500$ nm to $5000$ nm).

The size of a cancer cell (a eukaryotic human cell) typically ranges from $10$ $\mu$m to $30$ $\mu$m ($10000$ nm to $30000$ nm).

Comparing the sizes, the cancer cell is the largest among the given options.

Answer: Cancer cell

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is non reducing sugar?
questions.solution:
Reducing sugars are carbohydrates that can act as reducing agents due to the presence of a free aldehyde or ketone group. All monosaccharides, such as fructose, are reducing sugars.

In disaccharides, if the reducing groups of the monosaccharides are involved in forming the glycosidic bond, the sugar is non-reducing. In sucrose, the glycosidic linkage is formed between the C1 (anomeric carbon) of $\alpha$-D-glucose and the C2 (anomeric carbon) of $\beta$-D-fructose. Since both reducing groups are involved in the bond formation, sucrose does not have a free aldehyde or ketone group.

In contrast, maltose and lactose have free hemiacetal groups at one of their anomeric carbons, making them reducing sugars.

Therefore, sucrose is a non-reducing sugar.

Answer: Sucrose

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following exists as a zwitter ion ?
questions.solution:
Sulfanilic acid (p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid) contains both an acidic group ($-SO_3H$) and a basic group ($-NH_2$).

The strongly acidic sulfonic acid group donates a proton to the basic amino group, resulting in the formation of an internal salt or a dipolar ion known as a zwitterion.

The structure of the zwitterion is $p\text{-}^+H_3N-C_6H_4-SO_3^-$.

The other given compounds do not possess a combination of a sufficiently strong acidic group and a basic group to form a stable zwitterion.

Answer: Sulfanilic acid

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is a disaccharide ?
questions.solution:
Glucose and fructose are monosaccharides as they cannot be hydrolyzed further into simpler carbohydrates.

Sucrose is a disaccharide because on hydrolysis it yields two monosaccharide units: $\alpha$-D-glucose and $\beta$-D-fructose.

Starch is a polysaccharide composed of many glucose units.

Answer: Sucrose

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Name the polymer in which chains are formed by $\alpha$-1,4-glycosidic linkages between $\alpha$-glucose units and branches are formed by $\alpha$-1,6-glycosidic linkages.
questions.solution:
Amylopectin is a branched-chain polymer of $\alpha$-D-glucose units.

The linear chain is formed by $\alpha$-1,4-glycosidic linkages between the $\alpha$-D-glucose units.

The branching occurs through $\alpha$-1,6-glycosidic linkages.

Amylose is a linear polymer with only $\alpha$-1,4-glycosidic linkages.

Cellulose is a linear polymer of $\beta$-D-glucose units with $\beta$-1,4-glycosidic linkages.

Starch is a mixture of both amylose and amylopectin.

Answer: Amylopectin

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Match List I with List II
List I (CARBOHYDRATE)List II (TYPE)
(A). Glycogen(I). Oligosaccharide
(B). Stachyose(II). Monosaccharide
(C). Ribose(III). Polysaccharide
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
questions.solution:
Glycogen is a polymer of $\alpha$-D-glucose and is classified as a polysaccharide.

Stachyose is a tetrasaccharide, which yields four monosaccharide molecules on hydrolysis. It is classified as an oligosaccharide.

Ribose is a five-carbon sugar (aldopentose) that cannot be hydrolyzed further. It is classified as a monosaccharide.

Thus, the correct matching is (A) - (III), (B) - (I), (C) - (II).

Answer: (A) - (III), (B) - (I), (C) - (II)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is monomer used in cellulose, a straight chain polysaccharide?
questions.solution:
Cellulose is a straight-chain polysaccharide composed exclusively of $\beta$-D-glucose units.

These monomer units are joined together by $\beta$-1,4-glycosidic linkages between the C1 of one glucose unit and the C4 of the adjacent glucose unit.

Thus, the monomer used in cellulose is $\beta$-D-glucose.

Answer: $\beta$-D-glucose

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following reagents is used to identify one primary alcoholic group of glucose?
questions.solution:
Glucose contains one primary alcoholic group at the C-6 position.

When glucose is treated with dilute $\text{HNO}_3$, both the terminal aldehyde group and the primary alcoholic group are oxidized to carboxylic acid groups, resulting in the formation of saccharic acid (glucaric acid).

This specific oxidation reaction confirms the presence of a primary alcoholic group in glucose.

$\text{Br}_2$ water is a mild oxidizing agent that oxidizes only the aldehyde group to form gluconic acid, confirming the presence of an aldehyde group.

Acetic anhydride forms glucose pentaacetate, confirming the presence of five hydroxyl groups.

$\text{NH}_2\text{OH}$ forms an oxime, confirming the presence of a carbonyl group.

Answer: dilute $\text{HNO}_3$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the product formed when glucose is oxidized with concentrated nitric acid
questions.solution:
Glucose on oxidation with a strong oxidizing agent like concentrated nitric acid ($HNO_3$) undergoes oxidation at both the terminal aldehyde ($-CHO$) group and the terminal primary alcohol ($-CH_2OH$) group.

Both of these groups are converted into carboxylic acid ($-COOH$) groups, resulting in the formation of a dicarboxylic acid known as saccharic acid (or glucaric acid).

The reaction can be represented as:

$CHO-(CHOH)_4-CH_2OH \xrightarrow{HNO_3} COOH-(CHOH)_4-COOH$

Thus, the product formed is saccharic acid.

Answer: Saccharic acid

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which from following statements regarding biological importance of elements is NOT true?
options: $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ ions are required for contraction and stretching of muscles.
$\text{Mg}^{2+}$ ions are important for breaking of glucose and fats in protein synthesis.
$\text{K}^{+}$ ions are most abundant in extra cellular fluid.
$\text{Na}^{+}$ ions regulate water flow across the membrane.
questions.solution:
Sodium ions ($\text{Na}^{+}$) are the most abundant cations in the extracellular fluid (blood plasma and interstitial fluid), whereas potassium ions ($\text{K}^{+}$) are the most abundant cations in the intracellular fluid (within cell fluids).

Therefore, the statement that $\text{K}^{+}$ ions are most abundant in extracellular fluid is incorrect.

Answer: $\text{K}^{+}$ ions are most abundant in extra cellular fluid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What type of following peptides, the glycylalanine is?
questions.solution:
Glycylalanine is formed by the condensation of two amino acids, glycine and alanine, linked by a single peptide bond.

Since it contains exactly two amino acid residues, it is classified as a dipeptide.

Answer: Dipeptide

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Why does fructose reduce Fehling's solution?
options: Due to the presence of hydroxy group.
Due to the presence of aldehydic group
Due to the presence of ketonic group.
Due to the presence of alpha-hydroxy ketonic group
questions.solution:
Fructose is a monosaccharide containing a ketonic group at the second carbon and hydroxyl groups on adjacent carbons, making it an $\alpha$-hydroxy ketone.

Under the alkaline conditions of Fehling's solution, fructose undergoes Lobry de Bruyn-van Ekenstein rearrangement.

This rearrangement converts the $\alpha$-hydroxy ketone into an enediol intermediate, which further tautomerizes into an aldose (a mixture of glucose and mannose).

The newly formed aldehydic group then readily reduces Fehling's solution.

Thus, fructose reduces Fehling's solution due to the presence of the $\alpha$-hydroxy ketonic group.

Answer: Due to the presence of alpha-hydroxy ketonic group

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What type of compound the Keratin is?
questions.solution:
Keratin is a structural protein found in hair, nails, wool, and the outer layer of skin.

Based on their molecular shape, proteins are classified into fibrous and globular proteins.

Fibrous proteins consist of linear polypeptide chains that run parallel to each other and are held together by hydrogen and disulfide bonds, forming a fiber-like structure. They are generally insoluble in water.

Keratin and myosin are classic examples of fibrous proteins.

Answer: fibrous protein

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Formation of saccharic acid from glucose by oxidation with dilute $\text{HNO}_3$, confirms
options: the presence of aldehyde group in glucose.
glucose contains one primary alcoholic group.
the six carbon atoms of glucose are in a straight chain.
questions.solution:
Oxidation of glucose with dilute $\text{HNO}_3$ yields saccharic acid, which is a dicarboxylic acid.

Dilute $\text{HNO}_3$ is a strong oxidizing agent that oxidizes both the terminal aldehyde group and the terminal primary alcohol group of glucose into carboxylic acid groups.

This reaction specifically confirms the presence of a primary alcoholic group in glucose.

Answer: glucose contains one primary alcoholic group.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify glycosidic linkage present in maltose.
questions.solution:
Maltose is a disaccharide composed of two $\alpha$-D-glucose units.

The glycosidic linkage is formed between the C-1 of one $\alpha$-D-glucose unit and the C-4 of the other $\alpha$-D-glucose unit.

Thus, the glycosidic linkage present in maltose is $\alpha$-1,4.

Answer: $\alpha\text{-1,4}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds is obtained when glucose is heated with HI for a prolonged ?
questions.solution:
Glucose on prolonged heating with concentrated HI undergoes complete reduction to form n-hexane.

This reaction indicates that all the six carbon atoms in glucose are linked in a straight unbranched chain.

The reaction is given by:

$CHO-(CHOH)_4-CH_2OH \xrightarrow{HI, \Delta} CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-CH_3$ (n-hexane)

Answer: n-Hexane

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the product formed when glucose is treated with dil. $\text{HNO}_3$.
questions.solution:
Glucose on oxidation with dilute nitric acid ($\text{HNO}_3$) yields a dicarboxylic acid known as saccharic acid.

Dilute $\text{HNO}_3$ is a strong oxidizing agent that oxidizes both the terminal aldehyde group ($-\text{CHO}$) and the terminal primary alcohol group ($-\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$) of glucose into carboxylic acid groups ($-\text{COOH}$).

$\text{CHO}-(\text{CHOH})_4-\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{dil. HNO}_3} \text{COOH}-(\text{CHOH})_4-\text{COOH}$

Thus, the product formed is saccharic acid.

Answer: Saccharic acid

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is Zwitter ion of sulphanilic acid?
questions.solution:
Sulphanilic acid is $p$-aminobenzenesulphonic acid, which contains both an acidic group ($-SO_3H$) and a basic group ($-NH_2$).

In aqueous solution or in the solid state, the acidic $-SO_3H$ group donates a proton ($H^+$) to the basic $-NH_2$ group. This internal acid-base reaction leads to the formation of a dipolar ion, known as a zwitterion.

The resulting zwitterion has a positively charged ammonium group ($-NH_3^+$) and a negatively charged sulphonate group ($-SO_3^-$) at the para positions of the benzene ring.

Looking at the given options, the structure with $-NH_3^+$ and $-SO_3^-$ at para positions represents the zwitterion of sulphanilic acid.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the structure of alanine when pH is $11$.
questions.solution:
Alanine is an amino acid with an acidic carboxyl group and a basic amino group.

In a highly basic medium at pH $11$, the solution provides a large concentration of $\text{OH}^-$ ions.

Both the carboxyl group and the protonated amino group will lose their protons.

The $-\text{COOH}$ group deprotonates to form $-\text{COO}^-$, and the $-\text{NH}_3^+$ group deprotonates to form $-\text{NH}_2$.

Thus, alanine exists as an anion with the structure $\text{NH}_2\text{-CH(CH}_3\text{)-COO}^-$.

Answer: $\text{NH}_2\text{-CH(CH}_3\text{)-COO}^-$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the amino acid, NOT having nitrogen atom in a side chain.
questions.solution:
The structures of the side chains (R groups) for the given amino acids are as follows:

Asparagine: The side chain is $-CH_2CONH_2$, which contains an amide nitrogen.

Histidine: The side chain contains an imidazole ring, which has two nitrogen atoms.

Tryptophan: The side chain contains an indole ring, which has a nitrogen atom.

Threonine: The side chain is $-CH(OH)CH_3$, which contains only carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen atoms. It does not contain any nitrogen atom.

Therefore, Threonine is the amino acid that does not have a nitrogen atom in its side chain.

Answer: Threonine

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Most common secondary structure of protein Myosin in muscle and $\alpha$-keratin in hair are,
questions.solution:
Myosin in muscle and $\alpha$-keratin in hair are examples of fibrous proteins.

The most common secondary structure in both of these proteins is the $\alpha$-helix.

In $\alpha$-keratin, the polypeptide chains are coiled into a right-handed $\alpha$-helix.

Similarly, the tail region of myosin consists of two $\alpha$-helices coiled around each other to form a coiled-coil structure.

Thus, both proteins predominantly exhibit an $\alpha$-helical secondary structure.

Answer: both $\alpha$-helical

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the functional group / groups found in amino acid.
questions.solution:
Amino acids are organic molecules that serve as the building blocks of proteins.

The general structure of an alpha-amino acid consists of a central carbon atom (the alpha carbon) bonded to an amino group ($\text{-NH}_2$), a carboxyl group ($\text{-COOH}$), a hydrogen atom, and a variable side chain (R group).

Therefore, the functional groups characteristically found in all amino acids are the carboxyl group ($\text{COOH}$) and the amino group ($\text{NH}_2$).

Answer: COOH and $\text{NH}_2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following statements regarding fats is NOT true?
options: When fats are kept for longer time, develop rancidity.
Fats are triesters of fatty acids and propane-1,2,3-triol.
Chocolates develop fatty flavour due to release of lauric acid.
Release of fatty acids during hydrolysis of fats by water in food is one cause of rancidity.
questions.solution:
Fats are triesters of glycerol (propane-$1,2,3$-triol) and higher fatty acids.

When fats are stored for a long time, they undergo oxidation and hydrolysis, leading to rancidity.

Hydrolytic rancidity occurs when fats are hydrolyzed by moisture present in food, releasing free fatty acids which cause an unpleasant odour and taste.

Chocolates do not contain lauric acid in significant amounts; their fat content (cocoa butter) mainly consists of palmitic, stearic, and oleic acids. Lauric acid is predominantly found in coconut oil and palm kernel oil. Therefore, the statement that chocolates develop a fatty flavour due to the release of lauric acid is incorrect.

Answer: Chocolates develop fatty flavour due to release of lauric acid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the name of above nitrogen base.
questions.solution:
Based on the provided chemical structure:

1. The molecule is a single six-membered heterocyclic ring containing two nitrogen atoms, which identifies it as a pyrimidine base.
2. It has two carbonyl ($-C=O$) groups at positions 2 and 4, making it a 2,4-dioxopyrimidine.
3. There is no methyl group at the 5th position, which distinguishes it from thymine (5-methyluracil).
4. There is no amino group, which distinguishes it from cytosine (4-amino-2-hydroxypyrimidine).
5. Guanine is a purine base (consisting of two fused rings), so it can be ruled out immediately.

Therefore, the given structure is Uracil, which is a nitrogenous base found in RNA.

Answer: Uracil

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\text{O}_2$ gas has very low solubility in water. However, its solubility in blood is excessively high. This is because of
options: water is less viscous than blood
binding of $\text{O}_2$ molecule to hemoglobin in blood.
questions.solution:
Oxygen is a non-polar molecule, so it has very low solubility in polar solvents like water.

In blood, oxygen binds reversibly with the iron-containing protein hemoglobin present in red blood cells to form oxyhemoglobin.

This chemical binding significantly increases the oxygen-carrying capacity of blood compared to simple physical dissolution in water.

Answer: binding of $\text{O}_2$ molecule to hemoglobin in blood.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which from followings is true regarding structure of glucopyranose ?
options: Six membered heterocyclic ring containing five carbon and one oxygen atom
Five membered heterocyclic ring containing four carbon and one oxygen atom
Six membered heterocyclic ring containing six carbon atoms
Five membered homocyclic ring containing five carbon atoms
questions.solution:
Glucopyranose is the cyclic form of glucose.

The term 'pyranose' indicates that its structure is analogous to pyran.

Pyran is a six-membered heterocyclic ring consisting of five carbon atoms and one oxygen atom.

Therefore, glucopyranose is a six-membered heterocyclic ring containing five carbon atoms and one oxygen atom.

Answer: Six membered heterocyclic ring containing five carbon and one oxygen atom

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following acids is tricarboxylic acid?
questions.solution:
Lactic acid is a monocarboxylic acid with the structure $CH_3CH(OH)COOH$.

Citric acid is a tricarboxylic acid with the IUPAC name 2-hydroxypropane-1,2,3-tricarboxylic acid and the structure $HOOC-CH_2-C(OH)(COOH)-CH_2-COOH$.

Adipic acid is a dicarboxylic acid with the structure $HOOC-(CH_2)_4-COOH$.

Formic acid is a monocarboxylic acid with the structure $HCOOH$.

Therefore, citric acid is the only tricarboxylic acid among the given options.

Answer: Citric acid

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the process of making soap by the hydrolysis of fats and oils.
questions.solution:
Fats and oils are esters of higher fatty acids with glycerol.

The process of making soap involves the alkaline hydrolysis of these fats and oils using aqueous sodium hydroxide or potassium hydroxide.

This reaction produces glycerol and the sodium or potassium salts of the fatty acids, which are known as soaps.

This specific process of alkaline hydrolysis of esters to form soap is called saponification.

Answer: Saponification

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the trend of solubility of carboxylic acid in water.
options: Increases with increase in molecular mass.
Decreases with increase in molecular mass
Increases with increase in number of carbon atom
questions.solution:
Carboxylic acids are soluble in water due to the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds with water molecules.

As the molecular mass increases, the size of the alkyl group increases. The alkyl group is hydrophobic (water-repelling) in nature.

The larger the hydrophobic alkyl chain, the lesser the solubility of the carboxylic acid in water.

Therefore, the solubility of carboxylic acids in water decreases with an increase in molecular mass.

Answer: Decreases with increase in molecular mass

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the product of acid hydrolysis of propane nitrile.
questions.solution:
Propane nitrile is $CH_3CH_2CN$.

Complete acid hydrolysis of nitriles yields carboxylic acids with the same number of carbon atoms.

$CH_3CH_2CN + 2H_2O + H^+ \rightarrow CH_3CH_2COOH + NH_4^+$

The product formed is propanoic acid.

Answer: Propanoic acid

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following acid does NOT contain -COOH group?
questions.solution:
Lactic acid is 2-hydroxypropanoic acid, which has the formula $CH_3CH(OH)COOH$. It contains a -COOH group.

Picric acid is 2,4,6-trinitrophenol, which has the formula $C_6H_2(OH)(NO_2)_3$. It contains a phenolic -OH group but does not contain a -COOH group.

Benzoic acid is $C_6H_5COOH$. It contains a -COOH group.

Oxalic acid is ethanedioic acid, $HOOC-COOH$. It contains two -COOH groups.

Therefore, picric acid does not contain a -COOH group.

Answer: Picric acid

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following acid has the highest acid strength?
(A) Acetic acid
(B) fluoroacetic acid
(C) 4-Nitrobenzoic acid
(D) 4-Methyl Benzoic acid is
questions.solution:
Acid strength is directly proportional to the stability of the conjugate base, which increases with the presence of electron-withdrawing groups (-I, -R effects).

In fluoroacetic acid ($F-CH_2-COOH$), the highly electronegative fluorine atom exerts a strong -I effect from the adjacent $\alpha$-carbon, highly stabilizing the conjugate base. Its $pK_a$ is approximately $2.59$.

In 4-nitrobenzoic acid, the $-NO_2$ group exerts both -I and -R effects, but it is located farther away on the benzene ring. Its $pK_a$ is approximately $3.41$.

Acetic acid ($pK_a \approx 4.76$) and 4-methylbenzoic acid ($pK_a \approx 4.34$) have electron-donating groups (+I and hyperconjugation), which decrease acid strength.

Comparing the $pK_a$ values, fluoroacetic acid has the lowest $pK_a$ and is therefore the strongest acid among the given options.

Answer: (B)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the number of - COOH groups in a citric acid molecule?
questions.solution:
Citric acid is a tricarboxylic acid with the IUPAC name $2$-hydroxypropane-$1,2,3$-tricarboxylic acid.

The chemical structure of citric acid is:

$\text{HOOC}-\text{CH}_2-\text{C}(\text{OH})(\text{COOH})-\text{CH}_2-\text{COOH}$

From the structure, it is clear that there are $3$ carboxylic acid ($-\text{COOH}$) groups present in a citric acid molecule.

Answer: $3$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The order of strength of carboxylic acid is

Find the correct order.
questions.solution:
The acidic strength of a carboxylic acid depends on the stability of its conjugate base (carboxylate anion). Electron-withdrawing groups ($-I$ effect) stabilize the anion and increase acidic strength.

In the given compounds, the oxygen atom in the ring is more electronegative than carbon and exerts a $-I$ effect.

In structure (I), the oxygen atom is at the 4-position relative to the carbon bearing the $-\text{COOH}$ group.

In structure (II), there is no electronegative heteroatom in the ring, making it the least acidic among the three.

In structure (III), the oxygen atom is at the 3-position relative to the $-\text{COOH}$ group.

The $-I$ effect is distance-dependent; its strength decreases as the distance between the electron-withdrawing group and the acidic group increases. Since the oxygen atom in (III) is closer to the $-\text{COOH}$ group than in (I), the $-I$ effect is stronger in (III) compared to (I).

Therefore, the carboxylate anion of (III) is more stable than that of (I), making (III) a stronger acid than (I). Both (I) and (III) are stronger acids than (II) due to the presence of the electron-withdrawing oxygen atom.

The correct order of acidic strength is $\text{III} > \text{I} > \text{II}$.

Answer: $\text{III} > \text{I} > \text{II}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the organic compound having highest boiling point from following.
questions.solution:
The boiling point of organic compounds depends on the strength of intermolecular forces.

All the given compounds have comparable molecular masses (approximately $73-74 \text{ g/mol}$).

Amines form weaker hydrogen bonds compared to alcohols and carboxylic acids because nitrogen is less electronegative than oxygen.

Carboxylic acids form stronger intermolecular hydrogen bonds than alcohols and typically exist as dimers in the liquid phase, which significantly increases their effective molecular mass and boiling point.

Therefore, propanoic acid ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{-COOH}$) has the highest boiling point among the given compounds.

Answer: $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{-COOH}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following molecules shows $\text{sp}^3\text{d}$ hybridisation?
questions.solution:
The hybridisation of a central atom can be determined using the formula for steric number ($H$):

$H = \dfrac{1}{2}(V + M - C + A)$

where $V$ is the number of valence electrons on the central atom, $M$ is the number of monovalent atoms attached, $C$ is the cationic charge, and $A$ is the anionic charge.

For $\text{PCl}_5$:
Central atom is P ($V = 5$).
Number of monovalent Cl atoms ($M = 5$).
$H = \dfrac{1}{2}(5 + 5) = 5$
A steric number of $5$ corresponds to $\text{sp}^3\text{d}$ hybridisation.

For $\text{SO}_2$:
Central atom is S ($V = 6$).
Number of monovalent atoms ($M = 0$).
$H = \dfrac{1}{2}(6 + 0) = 3$ ($\text{sp}^2$ hybridisation).

For $\text{BF}_3$:
Central atom is B ($V = 3$).
Number of monovalent F atoms ($M = 3$).
$H = \dfrac{1}{2}(3 + 3) = 3$ ($\text{sp}^2$ hybridisation).

For $\text{CO}_2$:
Central atom is C ($V = 4$).
Number of monovalent atoms ($M = 0$).
$H = \dfrac{1}{2}(4 + 0) = 2$ ($\text{sp}$ hybridisation).

Thus, $\text{PCl}_5$ shows $\text{sp}^3\text{d}$ hybridisation.

Answer: $\text{PCl}_5$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify a pair of molecules having zero dipole moment.
questions.solution:
The dipole moment of a molecule depends on its geometry and the polarity of its bonds.

$\text{BF}_3$ has a symmetrical trigonal planar geometry, so its dipole moment is zero. However, $\text{HBr}$ is a polar diatomic molecule with a non-zero dipole moment.

$\text{CH}_4$ has a symmetrical tetrahedral geometry, so its dipole moment is zero. $\text{NF}_3$ has a trigonal pyramidal geometry with a lone pair on nitrogen, resulting in a non-zero dipole moment.

$\text{NH}_3$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ have trigonal pyramidal and bent geometries respectively, both possessing lone pairs that lead to non-zero dipole moments.

$\text{CCl}_4$ has a symmetrical tetrahedral geometry, causing the individual $\text{C-Cl}$ bond dipoles to cancel out, resulting in a zero dipole moment.

$\text{CO}_2$ has a linear geometry, where the two $\text{C=O}$ bond dipoles are equal and opposite, cancelling each other out to give a zero dipole moment.

Thus, both $\text{CCl}_4$ and $\text{CO}_2$ have zero dipole moments.

Answer: $\text{CCl}_4$ and $\text{CO}_2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the correct decreasing order of lattice enthalpy for metal fluorides from following.
questions.solution:
Lattice enthalpy is directly proportional to the product of charges of the ions and inversely proportional to the internuclear distance between them.

$U \propto \dfrac{q_1 q_2}{r_+ + r_-}$

For alkali metal fluorides, the charges on the ions are the same (+1 and -1). The ionic radius of alkali metal cations increases down the group in the order: $\text{Li}^+ < \text{Na}^+ < \text{K}^+$.

As the size of the cation increases, the internuclear distance increases, which leads to a decrease in the lattice enthalpy.

Therefore, the decreasing order of lattice enthalpy is $\text{LiF} > \text{NaF} > \text{KF}$.

Answer: $\text{LiF}>\text{NaF}>\text{KF}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following bonds is expected to be the most polar?
questions.solution:
The polarity of a covalent bond is directly proportional to the electronegativity difference between the two bonded atoms.

The electronegativity values of the given elements on the Pauling scale are approximately:
$\text{C} = 2.5$
$\text{H} = 2.1$
$\text{N} = 3.0$
$\text{O} = 3.5$

Calculating the electronegativity difference ($\Delta \text{EN}$) for each bond:
$\text{C--H}: \Delta \text{EN} = 2.5 - 2.1 = 0.4$
$\text{C--C}: \Delta \text{EN} = 2.5 - 2.5 = 0$
$\text{C--O}: \Delta \text{EN} = 3.5 - 2.5 = 1.0$
$\text{C--N}: \Delta \text{EN} = 3.0 - 2.5 = 0.5$

Since the electronegativity difference is maximum for the $\text{C--O}$ bond, it is the most polar among the given options.

Answer: $\text{C--O}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following species is paramagnetic?
questions.solution:
The magnetic nature of a species can be determined by its molecular orbital electronic configuration.

For $\text{N}_2$ (14 electrons): $\sigma_{1s}^{2} \sigma^{*}_{1s}^{2} \sigma_{2s}^{2} \sigma^{*}_{2s}^{2} \pi_{2p_x}^{2} = \pi_{2p_y}^{2} \sigma_{2p_z}^{2}$. All electrons are paired, so it is diamagnetic.

For $\text{Li}_2$ (6 electrons): $\sigma_{1s}^{2} \sigma^{*}_{1s}^{2} \sigma_{2s}^{2}$. All electrons are paired, so it is diamagnetic.

For $\text{F}_2$ (18 electrons): $\sigma_{1s}^{2} \sigma^{*}_{1s}^{2} \sigma_{2s}^{2} \sigma^{*}_{2s}^{2} \sigma_{2p_z}^{2} \pi_{2p_x}^{2} = \pi_{2p_y}^{2} \pi^{*}_{2p_x}^{2} = \pi^{*}_{2p_y}^{2}$. All electrons are paired, so it is diamagnetic.

For $\text{N}_2^{+}$ (13 electrons): $\sigma_{1s}^{2} \sigma^{*}_{1s}^{2} \sigma_{2s}^{2} \sigma^{*}_{2s}^{2} \pi_{2p_x}^{2} = \pi_{2p_y}^{2} \sigma_{2p_z}^{1}$. It has one unpaired electron, so it is paramagnetic.

Answer: $\text{N}_2^{+}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the number of bonding and antibonding electrons, respectively, in $\text{Li}_2$ molecule?
questions.solution:
The atomic number of Lithium ($\text{Li}$) is $3$.

Total number of electrons in $\text{Li}_2$ molecule is $6$.

The molecular orbital electronic configuration of $\text{Li}_2$ is $\sigma_{1s}^{2} \sigma^{*}_{1s}^{2} \sigma_{2s}^{2}$.

Number of bonding electrons ($N_b$) = $2 + 2 = 4$.

Number of antibonding electrons ($N_a$) = $2$.

Therefore, the number of bonding and antibonding electrons are $4$ and $2$, respectively.

Answer: $4$ and $2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the number of electrons in bonding molecular orbitals and antibonding molecular orbitals respectively in $\text{F}_2$ molecule?
questions.solution:
The total number of electrons in the F$_2$ molecule is $9 + 9 = 18$.

The molecular orbital electronic configuration of F$_2$ is:
$\sigma_{1s}^2 \sigma_{1s}^{*2} \sigma_{2s}^2 \sigma_{2s}^{*2} \sigma_{2p_z}^2 \pi_{2p_x}^2 \pi_{2p_y}^2 \pi_{2p_x}^{*2} \pi_{2p_y}^{*2}$

The number of electrons in bonding molecular orbitals ($N_b$) is the sum of electrons in $\sigma_{1s}$, $\sigma_{2s}$, $\sigma_{2p_z}$, $\pi_{2p_x}$, and $\pi_{2p_y}$ orbitals:

$N_b = 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 10$

The number of electrons in antibonding molecular orbitals ($N_a$) is the sum of electrons in $\sigma_{1s}^{*}$, $\sigma_{2s}^{*}$, $\pi_{2p_x}^{*}$, and $\pi_{2p_y}^{*}$ orbitals:

$N_a = 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 8$

Thus, the number of electrons in bonding and antibonding molecular orbitals are 10 and 8 respectively.

Answer: 10 and 8

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the hybridization in $\text{BF}_3$ molecule.
questions.solution:
The central atom in $\text{BF}_3$ is Boron (B), which has 3 valence electrons.

Boron forms 3 sigma bonds with 3 Fluorine (F) atoms and has 0 lone pairs.

Steric number = Number of sigma bonds + Number of lone pairs = $3 + 0 = 3$.

A steric number of 3 corresponds to $sp^2$ hybridization.

Answer: $sp^2$ hybridization

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify a hydride with highest boiling point from following?
questions.solution:
The hydrides of Group 16 elements are $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{H}_2\text{S}$, $\text{H}_2\text{Se}$, and $\text{H}_2\text{Te}$.

Generally, the boiling point of hydrides increases down the group due to an increase in molecular mass and van der Waals forces. However, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ has an exceptionally high boiling point due to the presence of strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding, which is absent in the other hydrides.

The order of boiling points is $\text{H}_2\text{S} < \text{H}_2\text{Se} < \text{H}_2\text{Te} < \text{H}_2\text{O}$.

Thus, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ has the highest boiling point.

Answer: $\text{H}_2\text{O}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which orbital overlap produces a $\sigma$ bond in HCl?
questions.solution:
The electronic configuration of H is $1s^1$.

The electronic configuration of Cl is $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^5$.

The unpaired electron in the hydrogen atom is present in the $1s$ orbital, which is spherically symmetrical.

The unpaired electron in the chlorine atom is present in one of the $3p$ orbitals, which is dumbbell-shaped.

The formation of the HCl molecule involves the axial overlap of the half-filled $1s$ orbital of the hydrogen atom with the half-filled $3p$ orbital of the chlorine atom.

This head-on overlap between an $s$ orbital and a $p$ orbital results in the formation of a $\sigma$ bond.

Thus, the orbital overlap that produces a $\sigma$ bond in HCl is $s-p$.

Answer: s$-$p

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The bond order of $\text{N}_2^+$ ion is:
questions.solution:
The total number of electrons in $\text{N}_2^+$ ion is $14 - 1 = 13$.

The molecular orbital electronic configuration of $\text{N}_2^+$ is:
$\sigma_{1s}^{2} \sigma^{*}_{1s}^{2} \sigma_{2s}^{2} \sigma^{*}_{2s}^{2} \pi_{2p_x}^{2} \pi_{2p_y}^{2} \sigma_{2p_z}^{1}$

Number of bonding electrons, $N_b = 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 = 9$
Number of antibonding electrons, $N_a = 2 + 2 = 4$

Bond order = $\dfrac{1}{2}(N_b - N_a)$

Bond order = $\dfrac{1}{2}(9 - 4) = \dfrac{5}{2} = 2.5$

Answer: $2.5$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the bond angle Cl-B-Cl and molecular geometry of $\text{BCl}_3$ molecule?
questions.solution:
The central atom in $\text{BCl}_3$ is Boron (B).

The number of valence electrons in Boron is $3$. It forms $3$ single bonds with $3$ Chlorine atoms.

Number of bond pairs = $3$
Number of lone pairs = $0$

Steric number = Bond pairs + Lone pairs = $3 + 0 = 3$

A steric number of $3$ corresponds to $sp^2$ hybridization.

Since there are no lone pairs on the central atom, the molecular geometry is trigonal planar.

The bond angle for a regular trigonal planar geometry is $120^\circ$.

Answer: $120^\circ$ and trigonal planar

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following is NOT an example of an expanded octate?
questions.solution:
In $\text{PCl}_5$, the central atom phosphorus forms $5$ single bonds, having $10$ valence electrons around it, which is an expanded octet.

In $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, the central atom sulfur forms $6$ bonds ($2$ double bonds and $2$ single bonds), having $12$ valence electrons around it, which is an expanded octet.

In $\text{SF}_6$, the central atom sulfur forms $6$ single bonds, having $12$ valence electrons around it, which is an expanded octet.

In $\text{CH}_4$, the central atom carbon forms $4$ single bonds, having exactly $8$ valence electrons around it. Carbon belongs to the second period and lacks vacant d-orbitals, so it cannot expand its octet.

Thus, $\text{CH}_4$ is not an example of an expanded octet.

Answer: $\text{CH}_4$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is the decreasing order of covalent character of alkali metal halides?
questions.solution:
According to Fajan's rules, the covalent character of an ionic compound increases with the polarizability of the anion.

The polarizability of an anion increases with an increase in its size.

For halide ions, the ionic size increases down the group in the order: $\text{F}^{-} < \text{Cl}^{-} < \text{Br}^{-} < \text{I}^{-}$.

Therefore, the polarizability of the halide ions and the covalent character of the corresponding alkali metal halides increase in the same order.

The decreasing order of covalent character is $\text{MI} > \text{MBr} > \text{MCl} > \text{MF}$.

Answer: $\text{MI} > \text{MBr} > \text{MCl} > \text{MF}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds has the highest boiling point?
questions.solution:
Boiling point depends on the strength of intermolecular forces.

$\text{CH}_3\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_3$ is non-polar and has only weak London dispersion forces.

$\text{CH}_3\text{-O-CH}_3$ and $\text{CH}_3\text{-Cl}$ are polar and have dipole-dipole interactions, but their dipole moments are relatively small.

$\text{CH}_3\text{-CN}$ has a highly polar $-\text{CN}$ group, resulting in a very high dipole moment and strong dipole-dipole interactions.

Due to the strongest intermolecular forces, $\text{CH}_3\text{-CN}$ has the highest boiling point among the given compounds.

Answer: $\text{CH}_3\text{-CN}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For which of the following solids does solubility decrease with an increase in temperature?
questions.solution:
The effect of temperature on the solubility of a solid depends on whether the dissolution process is endothermic or exothermic.

According to Le Chatelier's principle, if the dissolution is endothermic, solubility increases with temperature. If the dissolution is exothermic, solubility decreases with temperature.

The dissolution of potassium nitrate, sodium nitrate, and potassium chloride in water are endothermic processes, so their solubilities increase as temperature rises.

For sodium sulphate, above its transition temperature, it exists in its anhydrous form and its dissolution is an exothermic process. Therefore, its solubility decreases with an increase in temperature.

Answer: Sodium sulphate.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The combination of $\text{NO}_2(g)$ and $\text{CO}(g)$ occurs in two steps as follows: What is the rate law for the reaction?
$2\text{NO}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{NO}_3$ $(k_1)$ - slow
$\text{NO}_3 + \text{CO} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{NO}_2$ $(k_2)$ - fast
questions.solution:
The rate of a complex reaction is determined by the slowest step, which is known as the rate-determining step.

In the given mechanism, the first step is the slow step:
$2\text{NO}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{NO}_3$

For an elementary step, the rate law is written directly from its stoichiometry according to the law of mass action.

Rate $= k_1[\text{NO}_2]^2$

Answer: Rate $= k_1[\text{NO}_2]^2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The slope of the graph $[\text{A}]_t$ against time for zero order reaction is
questions.solution:
For a zero order reaction, the integrated rate law is given by:

$[\text{A}]_t = -kt + [\text{A}]_0$

Comparing this equation with the equation of a straight line $y = mx + c$, we get $y = [\text{A}]_t$ and $x = t$.

The slope of the graph $m$ is $-k$ and the y-intercept $c$ is $[\text{A}]_0$.

Therefore, the slope of the graph of $[\text{A}]_t$ against time is $-k$.

Answer: $-k$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the reaction
$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{Br}_{(l)} \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_{4(g)} + \text{HBr}$, $r = K(\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{Br})$
What is order and molecularity of the reaction respectively ?
questions.solution:
The rate law is given as $r = K[\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{Br}]$.

The order of a reaction is the sum of the exponents of the concentration terms in the rate law. Since the exponent of $[\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{Br}]$ is $1$, the order of the reaction is $1$.

The molecularity of an elementary reaction is the number of reactant molecules taking part in the reaction. In the given reaction, only one molecule of $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{Br}$ is reacting, so the molecularity is $1$.

Thus, the order and molecularity are $1$ and $1$ respectively.

Answer: $1, 1$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The decomposition of $\text{N}_2\text{O}_{5(g)}$ occurs as: $2\ \text{N}_2\text{O}_{5(g)} \rightarrow 4\ \text{NO}_{2(g)} + \text{O}_{2(g)}$.
If initial concentration of $\text{N}_2\text{O}_{5(g)}$ is $1.63 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{mol L}^{-1}$ and after $400$ seconds it is $1.36 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{mol L}^{-1}$, calculate the rate of reaction.
questions.solution:
The rate of reaction is given by the expression:

$\text{Rate} = -\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\Delta [\text{N}_2\text{O}_5]}{\Delta t}$

$\Delta [\text{N}_2\text{O}_5] = 1.36 \times 10^{-2} - 1.63 \times 10^{-2} = -0.27 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{mol L}^{-1}$

$\Delta t = 400\ \text{s}$

$\text{Rate} = -\dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{-0.27 \times 10^{-2}}{400} \right)$

$\text{Rate} = \dfrac{0.27 \times 10^{-2}}{800} = 3.375 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{mol L}^{-1}\ \text{s}^{-1}$

Rounding off, the rate of reaction is $3.38 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{mol L}^{-1}\ \text{s}^{-1}$.

Answer: $3.38 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{mol L}^{-1}\ \text{s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following statements represents rate of reaction?
options: Change in temperature per unit time
Change in concentration of reactants or products per unit time
Change in volume of the reaction mixture
Change in color of the solution
questions.solution:
The rate of a chemical reaction is defined as the change in the concentration of any one of the reactants or products per unit time.

Mathematically, Rate $= \pm \dfrac{\Delta C}{\Delta t}$, where $\Delta C$ is the change in concentration and $\Delta t$ is the time interval.

Answer: Change in concentration of reactants or products per unit time

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
What is the order of reaction for the following?
$2\text{H}_2\text{O}_2(l) \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) + \text{O}_2(g)$
questions.solution:
The decomposition of hydrogen peroxide is a well-known example of a first-order reaction.

The rate law for this reaction is given by:

$\text{Rate} = k[\text{H}_2\text{O}_2]$

Since the power of the concentration term in the rate law is $1$, the order of the reaction is first.

Answer: First

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following are not used for the treatment of humans?
questions.solution:
Antiseptics are chemical substances which prevent the growth of microorganisms or kill them and are safe to be applied to living tissues such as wounds, cuts, ulcers, and diseased skin surfaces.

Disinfectants are chemical substances which kill microorganisms but are not safe to be applied to living tissues. They are generally applied to inanimate objects such as floors, drainage systems, and instruments.

Antimalarials and antibiotics are drugs used for the treatment of diseases in humans.

Therefore, disinfectants are not used for the treatment of humans.

Answer: Disinfectants

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the metal nanoparticle that acts as a highly effective bacterial disinfectant in the water purification process.
questions.solution:
Silver nanoparticles are widely known for their strong antimicrobial and antibacterial properties. They are extensively used as highly effective bacterial disinfectants in water purification processes to eliminate harmful pathogens.

Carbon black is a non-metal, while aluminium and copper are not primarily used as highly effective bacterial disinfectants in this context.

Answer: silver

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the correct chemical name of an insecticide DDT?
questions.solution:
DDT is a well-known insecticide.

The full form of DDT is Dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane.

Its IUPAC name is 1,1,1-trichloro-2,2-bis(4-chlorophenyl)ethane.

Answer: Dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the chemical used to slow down the browning action of cut fruit.
questions.solution:
The browning of cut fruits is primarily due to enzymatic oxidation, specifically the action of the enzyme polyphenol oxidase.

Sulfur dioxide ($\text{SO}_2$) acts as an antioxidant and a preservative. It inhibits the activity of polyphenol oxidase and prevents the oxidation process, thereby slowing down the browning action of cut fruits.

Answer: $\text{SO}_2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the role of aspirin?
questions.solution:
Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) belongs to the class of drugs known as non-narcotic analgesics.

It is primarily used to relieve skeletal pain, such as that due to arthritis, by inhibiting the synthesis of chemicals called prostaglandins, which stimulate inflammation in the tissues and cause pain. It also has antipyretic (fever-reducing) properties.

Thus, the primary role of aspirin is as an analgesic.

Answer: Analgesics

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the structural formula of aspirin.
questions.solution:
Aspirin is the common name for acetylsalicylic acid. It is formed by the acetylation of the hydroxyl group of salicylic acid.

Its chemical structure consists of a benzene ring substituted with a carboxylic acid group ($-COOH$) and an acetoxy group ($-OCOCH_3$) at the ortho position relative to each other.

Looking at the given options:
The first structure is salicylic acid (2-hydroxybenzoic acid).
The second structure is methyl salicylate.
The third structure is acetylsalicylic acid, which is aspirin.
The fourth structure is dimethyl phthalate.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Clove is recommended for toothaches because
options: Curcumin present in it possesses antiseptic properties.
Eugenol, present in it, exhibits analgesic and antimicrobial properties.
Methyl salicylate present in it possesses analgesic properties.
The gallic acid present in it exhibits antimicrobial and antioxidant properties.
questions.solution:
Clove is recommended for toothaches primarily because it contains eugenol. Eugenol is an essential oil that exhibits both analgesic (pain-relieving) and antimicrobial properties, making it highly effective in numbing dental pain and preventing infections.

Answer: Eugenol, present in it, exhibits analgesic and antimicrobial properties.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the structure of paracetamol from the following.
questions.solution:
Paracetamol is also known as acetaminophen. Its IUPAC name is N-(4-hydroxyphenyl)acetamide.

The structure consists of a benzene ring substituted with a hydroxyl group ($-OH$) and an acetamide group ($-NHCOCH_3$) at the para position relative to each other.

Analyzing the given options:
The first structure is a urea derivative.
The second structure is a carbamic acid derivative.
The third structure has an extra methyl group on the benzene ring.
The fourth structure has exactly a hydroxyl group and an acetamide group para to each other on a benzene ring, which corresponds to paracetamol.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the term used for the process of alkaline hydrolysis of natural oils and fats to produce soap.
questions.solution:
Natural oils and fats are esters of higher fatty acids with glycerol. The process of alkaline hydrolysis of these oils and fats using aqueous alkali (like $\text{NaOH}$ or $\text{KOH}$) produces sodium or potassium salts of fatty acids, which are known as soaps, along with glycerol as a byproduct.

This specific process of making soap through the alkaline hydrolysis of esters is termed saponification.

Answer: Saponification

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q:
Match the following
List IList II
A. TechnetiumI. Non-metal
B. FluorineII. Transition metal
C. TelluriumIII. Lanthanoid
D. DysprosiumIV. Metalloid
questions.solution:
Technetium (Tc) is a d-block element and is classified as a transition metal.

Fluorine (F) is a p-block element and is classified as a non-metal.

Tellurium (Te) is a p-block element and is classified as a metalloid.

Dysprosium (Dy) is an f-block element and is classified as a lanthanoid.

Therefore, the correct matching is A-II, B-I, C-IV, D-III.

Answer: A-II, B-I, C-IV, D-III

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Which of the following properties increases from fluorine to iodine in group?
questions.solution:
As we move down the group from fluorine to iodine, the number of electron shells increases. Consequently, the atomic and ionic radii increase.

Electronegativity decreases down the group due to the increase in atomic size.

Volatility decreases down the group because the magnitude of van der Waals forces of attraction increases with an increase in molecular size and mass (fluorine is a gas, whereas iodine is a solid).

Oxidizing power decreases down the group because the standard reduction potential decreases from fluorine to iodine.

Thus, only the ionic radius increases from fluorine to iodine.

Answer: Ionic radius

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Identify the metalloid element from the following.
questions.solution:
Metalloids are elements that have properties intermediate between those of metals and non-metals.

The common metalloids are Boron ($\text{B}$), Silicon ($\text{Si}$), Germanium ($\text{Ge}$), Arsenic ($\text{As}$), Antimony ($\text{Sb}$), Tellurium ($\text{Te}$), and Polonium ($\text{Po}$).

Among the given options, Oxygen ($\text{O}$), Sulfur ($\text{S}$), and Chlorine ($\text{Cl}$) are non-metals.

Tellurium ($\text{Te}$) is a metalloid.

Answer: $\text{Te}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Which among the following elements does NOT exhibit $-2$ oxidation state?
questions.solution:
The given elements belong to Group 16 of the periodic table.

The tendency to exhibit a $-2$ oxidation state decreases down the group due to an increase in atomic size, a decrease in electronegativity, and an increase in metallic character.

Oxygen, Selenium, and Tellurium are non-metals/metalloids and readily exhibit a $-2$ oxidation state.

Polonium (Po) is a radioactive metal with low electronegativity. Therefore, it hardly exhibits a $-2$ oxidation state. Its common oxidation states are $+2$ and $+4$.

Answer: Po

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Which has the maximum electropositive character?
questions.solution:
Electropositive character refers to the tendency of an element to lose electrons and form positive ions. It increases down a group and decreases across a period from left to right in the periodic table.

Alkali metals (Group 1) are the most electropositive elements in their respective periods. Among the given options, $\text{Cs}$ (Cesium) is an alkali metal located at the bottom of Group 1 (Period 6). It has the lowest ionization enthalpy among the given elements, making it the most electropositive.

$\text{Ba}$ is an alkaline earth metal (Group 2) and is less electropositive than $\text{Cs}$. $\text{Cu}$ and $\text{Cr}$ are transition metals and have significantly lower electropositive character compared to s-block elements.

Answer: $\text{Cs}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: The hydride with lowest acid strength among the following is
questions.solution:
The given compounds are hydrides of Group 16 elements.

As we move down the group, the atomic size of the central atom increases. This causes the bond length of the E-H bond (where E is the Group 16 element) to increase and the bond dissociation enthalpy to decrease.

Consequently, the tendency to release an $H^+$ ion increases down the group, leading to an increase in acidic strength.

The order of acidic strength of Group 16 hydrides is $H_2O < H_2S < H_2Se < H_2Te$.

Therefore, $H_2O$ has the lowest acid strength.

Answer: $\text{H}_2\text{O}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $[\text{Ti(H}_2\text{O)}_6]^{2+}$ is a colored compound where as $[\text{Zn(H}_2\text{O)}_6]^{2+}$ is colorless, because
options: Titanium has completely filled 3d orbitals and Zinc has partially filled 3d orbitals.
Zinc has completely filled 3d orbitals and Titanium has partially filled 3d orbitals.
questions.solution:
The atomic number of Titanium (Ti) is $22$. Its ground state electronic configuration is $[\text{Ar}] 3d^{2} 4s^{2}$.

In the complex $[\text{Ti(H}_2\text{O)}_6]^{2+}$, Titanium is in the $+2$ oxidation state. The electronic configuration of $\text{Ti}^{2+}$ is $[\text{Ar}] 3d^{2}$. Since it has partially filled $3d$ orbitals, it can undergo $d-d$ transitions, which are responsible for its color.

The atomic number of Zinc (Zn) is $30$. Its ground state electronic configuration is $[\text{Ar}] 3d^{10} 4s^{2}$.

In the complex $[\text{Zn(H}_2\text{O)}_6]^{2+}$, Zinc is in the $+2$ oxidation state. The electronic configuration of $\text{Zn}^{2+}$ is $[\text{Ar}] 3d^{10}$. Since it has completely filled $3d$ orbitals, $d-d$ transitions are not possible, making the complex colorless.

Thus, Zinc has completely filled $3d$ orbitals and Titanium has partially filled $3d$ orbitals.

Answer: Zinc has completely filled 3d orbitals and Titanium has partially filled 3d orbitals.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is coordination number of central metal ion in cisplatin ?
questions.solution:
Cisplatin is cis-diamminedichloridoplatinum(II), with the chemical formula $[Pt(NH_3)_2Cl_2]$.

The central metal ion is $Pt^{2+}$, which is coordinated to two ammonia ($NH_3$) ligands and two chloride ($Cl^-$) ligands.

Since both $NH_3$ and $Cl^-$ are monodentate ligands, the total number of coordinate bonds formed with the central metal ion is $2 + 2 = 4$.

Thus, the coordination number of the central metal ion in cisplatin is $4$.

Answer: $4$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following complexes exhibits solvate isomerism?
questions.solution:
Solvate isomerism (or hydrate isomerism when water is the solvent) occurs when the solvent molecules are directly bonded to the metal ion or are present as free solvent molecules in the crystal lattice.

In $[\text{Cr(H}_2\text{O)}_6]\text{Cl}_3$ and $[\text{Cr(H}_2\text{O)}_5\text{Cl}]\text{Cl}_2 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}$, the number of water molecules inside and outside the coordination sphere is different. Thus, they exhibit solvate isomerism.

$[\text{Co(NH}_3)_6]\ [\text{Cr(CN)}_6]$ and $[\text{Cr(NH}_3)_6]\ [\text{Co(CN)}_6]$ exhibit coordination isomerism.

$[\text{Co(NH}_3)_5\text{Br}]\text{SO}_4$ and $[\text{Co(NH}_3)_5\ \text{SO}_4]\text{Br}$ exhibit ionization isomerism.

$[\text{Co(NH}_3)_5(\text{NO}_2)]^{2+}$ and $[\text{Co(NH}_3)_5(\text{ONO})]^{2+}$ exhibit linkage isomerism.

Answer: $[\text{Cr(H}_2\text{O)}_6]\text{Cl}_3$ and $[\text{Cr(H}_2\text{O)}_5\text{Cl}]\text{Cl}_2 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What type of ligand the ethylenediamine is?
questions.solution:
Ethylenediamine ($H_2N-CH_2-CH_2-NH_2$) contains two nitrogen atoms, each possessing a lone pair of electrons.

It can donate these two pairs of electrons to a central metal atom or ion, forming two coordinate bonds.

Therefore, ethylenediamine is a bidentate ligand.

Answer: Bidentatae

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following isomers is used in the treatment of cancer?
questions.solution:
Cisplatin, chemically known as cis-diamminedichloridoplatinum(II) or cis-$[\text{Pt}(\text{NH}_3)_2\text{Cl}_2]$, is a well-known coordination compound widely used as a chemotherapy medication for the treatment of various types of cancer.

The trans isomer, trans-$[\text{Pt}(\text{NH}_3)_2\text{Cl}_2]$, does not exhibit the same pharmacological effect and is not used as an anti-cancer drug.

Answer: cis-$[\text{Pt}(\text{NH}_3)_2\text{Cl}_2]$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the geometry and magnetic behavior of the complex $[\text{Ni}(\text{CO})_4]$.
questions.solution:
The oxidation state of $\text{Ni}$ in $[\text{Ni}(\text{CO})_4]$ is $0$.

The electronic configuration of $\text{Ni}(0)$ is $[\text{Ar}] 3d^8 4s^2$.

Since $\text{CO}$ is a strong field ligand, it causes the pairing of electrons. The two $4s$ electrons are forced into the $3d$ orbitals, resulting in a completely filled $3d^{10}$ configuration.

The empty $4s$ and three $4p$ orbitals undergo $sp^3$ hybridization to accommodate the four electron pairs from the four $\text{CO}$ ligands.

Due to $sp^3$ hybridization, the geometry of the complex is tetrahedral.

Since all electrons are paired, the complex is diamagnetic.

Answer: tetrahedral geometry and diamagnetic

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct match from the following for coordinate complex (column A) and respective geometry (column B)
Column A
Coordination complex		Column B
Geometry
(A). $[\text{Ni}(\text{CN})_4]^{-2}$(I). Trigonal bipyramidal
(B). $[\text{Ag}(\text{NH}_3)_2]^+$(II). Linear
(C). $[\text{Ni}(\text{Cl})_4]^{2-}$(III). Tetrahedral
(D). $[\text{Fe}(\text{CO})_5]$(IV). Square planar
questions.solution:
For $[\text{Ni}(\text{CN})_4]^{2-}$, $\text{Ni}^{2+}$ has a $3d^8$ configuration. $\text{CN}^-$ is a strong field ligand, causing pairing of electrons. The hybridization is $dsp^2$, resulting in a square planar geometry. Thus, (A) matches with (IV).

For $[\text{Ag}(\text{NH}_3)_2]^+$, $\text{Ag}^+$ has a $4d^{10}$ configuration. With a coordination number of $2$, the hybridization is $sp$, resulting in a linear geometry. Thus, (B) matches with (II).

For $[\text{Ni}(\text{Cl})_4]^{2-}$, $\text{Ni}^{2+}$ has a $3d^8$ configuration. $\text{Cl}^-$ is a weak field ligand, so no pairing occurs. The hybridization is $sp^3$, resulting in a tetrahedral geometry. Thus, (C) matches with (III).

For $[\text{Fe}(\text{CO})_5]$, $\text{Fe}$ is in the $0$ oxidation state with a $3d^6 4s^2$ configuration. $\text{CO}$ is a strong field ligand, causing pairing of electrons to give a $3d^8$ configuration. The hybridization is $dsp^3$, resulting in a trigonal bipyramidal geometry. Thus, (D) matches with (I).

The correct match is (A) - (IV), (B) - (II), (C) - (III), (D) - (I).

Answer: (A) - (IV), (B) - (II), (C) - (III), (D) - (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which type of complex is Diamminedichloroplatinum(II)?
questions.solution:
The given complex is Diamminedichloroplatinum(II).

The chemical formula of the complex is $[Pt(NH_3)_2Cl_2]$.

The oxidation state of platinum (Pt) is $+2$.

The ligands are two ammine ($NH_3$) molecules, which are neutral, and two chloride ($Cl^-$) ions, which have a charge of $-1$ each.

Total charge on the complex sphere = $+2 + 2(0) + 2(-1) = 0$.

Since the net charge on the coordination sphere is zero, it is a neutral complex.

Answer: Neutral

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which atoms from EDTA are used to bind to metal ions during formation of complex?
options: Four nitrogen and two oxygen atoms
Three nitrogen and three oxygen atoms
Four oxygen and two nitrogen atoms
Three oxygen, two nitrogen and one magnesium atoms
questions.solution:
EDTA (Ethylenediaminetetraacetate) is a hexadentate ligand.

Its chemical structure contains two nitrogen atoms and four carboxylate groups.

During the formation of a complex with a metal ion, it donates electron pairs from the two nitrogen atoms and the four oxygen atoms of the carboxylate groups.

Thus, four oxygen and two nitrogen atoms are used to bind to metal ions.

Answer: Four oxygen and two nitrogen atoms

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of following ligands is present in a metal carbonyl complex?
questions.solution:
Metal carbonyl complexes are coordination compounds in which transition metals are bonded to carbon monoxide ($\text{CO}$) ligands.

Therefore, the ligand present in a metal carbonyl complex is $\text{CO}$.

Answer: $\text{CO}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the correct IUPAC name of $\text{K}_3[\text{Fe(CN)}_6]$?
questions.solution:
The given coordination compound is $\text{K}_3[\text{Fe(CN)}_6]$.

The cation is potassium and the complex anion is $[\text{Fe(CN)}_6]^{3-}$.

Let the oxidation state of iron (Fe) be $x$.

The charge on each cyanide (CN) ligand is $-1$.

Equating the sum of oxidation states to the charge on the complex ion:

$x + 6(-1) = -3$

$x - 6 = -3$

$x = +3$

The complex anion contains six cyanide ligands, which are named as 'hexacyano'.

Since the complex is an anion, the suffix '-ate' is added to the Latin name of iron, making it 'ferrate'.

The oxidation state is written in Roman numerals in parentheses as (III).

Combining these parts, the IUPAC name is Potassium hexacyanoferrate(III).

Answer: Potassium hexacyanoferrate(III)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is always a bidentate ligand?
questions.solution:
Ammonia ($NH_3$), water ($H_2O$), and carbon monoxide ($CO$) are monodentate ligands because they donate only one pair of electrons to the central metal atom or ion.

Ethylenediamine ($H_2N-CH_2-CH_2-NH_2$) is a bidentate ligand because it has two nitrogen atoms, each with a lone pair of electrons, which can simultaneously coordinate to the central metal atom or ion.

Answer: Ethylenediamine

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following coordination compounds is not likely to show optical isomerism?
questions.solution:
Optical isomerism is generally shown by octahedral complexes that lack elements of symmetry (plane of symmetry, center of symmetry).

Let us analyze each complex:

Option (1): $[NiCl_2(en)_2]$ can exist in cis and trans isomeric forms. The cis form lacks a plane of symmetry and is optically active. The trans form has a plane of symmetry and is optically inactive. Therefore, the complex as a whole can show optical isomerism.

Option (2): $[Co(en)_3]^{3+}$ is an octahedral complex with three bidentate ligands. It lacks any plane or center of symmetry. Thus, it exists as a pair of non-superimposable mirror images (enantiomers) and shows optical isomerism.

Option (3): $[Cu(NH_3)_4]^{2+}$ is a typical example of a square planar complex. Square planar complexes contain a molecular plane of symmetry (the plane containing the metal ion and the four ligands). Because of this plane of symmetry, they do not show optical isomerism.

Option (4): $[PtCl_2(en)_2]^{2+}$ is an octahedral complex similar to the first option. Its cis-isomer is optically active, while the trans-isomer is optically inactive. Therefore, it can show optical isomerism.

Thus, the square planar complex $[Cu(NH_3)_4]^{2+}$ is not likely to show optical isomerism.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Find the hybridization and number of coordinate bonds formed in tetracyanonickelate(II) ion respectively.
questions.solution:
The given complex ion is tetracyanonickelate(II), which is $[Ni(CN)_4]^{2-}$.

The central metal ion is Ni in +2 oxidation state. The atomic number of Ni is 28, so the electronic configuration of $Ni^{2+}$ is $[Ar] 3d^8$.

Since $CN^-$ is a strong field ligand, it causes the pairing of the $3d$ electrons. The 8 electrons pair up to occupy four $3d$ orbitals, leaving one $3d$ orbital empty.

The empty orbitals available for hybridization are one $3d$, one $4s$, and two $4p$ orbitals. Thus, the hybridization is $dsp^2$, resulting in a square planar geometry.

Since there are four monodentate $CN^-$ ligands, each donates a pair of electrons to the central metal ion, forming a total of 4 coordinate bonds.

Therefore, the hybridization is $dsp^2$ and the number of coordinate bonds is 4.

Answer: $dsp^2$ and 4 coordinate bonds.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the IUPAC name of $\text{K}_3[\text{Co}(\text{C}_2\text{O}_4)_3]$ ?
questions.solution:
The given coordination compound is $\text{K}_3[\text{Co}(\text{C}_2\text{O}_4)_3]$.

The cation is potassium, which is written first.

The complex part is an anion, $[\text{Co}(\text{C}_2\text{O}_4)_3]^{3-}$. For anionic complexes, the name of the central metal ends with the suffix '-ate'. Thus, cobalt becomes cobaltate.

The ligand is oxalate ($\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}$), which is named 'oxalato'. Since there are three oxalate ligands, the prefix 'tri' is used, giving 'trioxalato'.

Let the oxidation state of cobalt be $x$.
The charge on one oxalate ligand is $-2$.
The sum of oxidation states in the complex anion is equal to its net charge:
$x + 3(-2) = -3$
$x - 6 = -3$
$x = +3$

The oxidation state of cobalt is (III).

Combining these parts, the IUPAC name of the compound is Potassium trioxalatocobaltate(III).

Answer: Potassium trioxalatocobaltate(III)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What type of ligand the oxalate ion is?
questions.solution:
The oxalate ion is $C_2O_4^{2-}$.

It contains two donor oxygen atoms which can simultaneously coordinate to a central metal atom or ion.

Since it has two donor sites, it acts as a bidentate ligand.

Answer: Bidentate

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify from the following a coordinate complex containing neutral ligand in it.
questions.solution:
Pentacarbonyliron(0) is $[Fe(CO)_5]$. The ligand is carbonyl ($CO$), which is a neutral molecule.

Hexacyanoferrate(II) ion is $[Fe(CN)_6]^{4-}$. The ligand is cyanide ($CN^-$), which is anionic.

Trioxalatocobaltate(III) ion is $[Co(C_2O_4)_3]^{3-}$. The ligand is oxalate ($C_2O_4^{2-}$), which is anionic.

Sodium hexanitrocobaltate(III) is $Na_3[Co(NO_2)_6]$. The ligand is nitro ($NO_2^-$), which is anionic.

Thus, pentacarbonyliron(0) contains a neutral ligand.

Answer: Pentacarbonyliron(0)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The IUPAC name of $[\text{CoCl(NH}_3)_5]\text{Cl}_2$ is:
questions.solution:
The given complex is $[\text{CoCl(NH}_3)_5]\text{Cl}_2$.

The coordination sphere is the cation $[\text{CoCl(NH}_3)_5]^{2+}$ and the counter ion is the chloride anion $\text{Cl}^-$.

Let the oxidation state of cobalt be $x$.

$x + (-1) + 5(0) = +2 \Rightarrow x = +3$

The ligands present in the coordination sphere are ammine ($\text{NH}_3$) and chloro ($\text{Cl}^-$). According to alphabetical order, ammine is written before chloro.

Since there are five ammine ligands and one chloro ligand, the prefix is pentaamminechloro.

The central metal is in a cationic complex, so it is named cobalt, followed by its oxidation state in Roman numerals, which is (III).

The counter ion is named chloride (the number of counter ions is not indicated by prefixes like di or tri).

Combining these parts, the IUPAC name of the complex is pentaamminechlorocobalt(III) chloride.

Answer: Pentaamminechlorocobalt(III) chloride

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following coordinate complexes contains one or more neutral ligand / ligands in its coordination sphere?
questions.solution:
Let us analyze the ligands present in the coordination sphere of each complex:

Potassium trioxalatoaluminate(III): $K_3[Al(C_2O_4)_3]$ contains oxalato ligands ($C_2O_4^{2-}$), which are anionic.

Pentaammineaquacobalt(III) iodide: $[Co(NH_3)_5(H_2O)]I_3$ contains ammine ($NH_3$) and aqua ($H_2O$) ligands, both of which are neutral molecules.

Sodium hexanitrocobaltate(III): $Na_3[Co(NO_2)_6]$ contains nitro ligands ($NO_2^{-}$), which are anionic.

Hexacyanoferrate(II) ion: $[Fe(CN)_6]^{4-}$ contains cyano ligands ($CN^{-}$), which are anionic.

Thus, pentaammineaquacobalt(III) iodide is the only complex containing neutral ligands in its coordination sphere.

Answer: Pentaammineaquacobalt(III) iodide

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Why aqueous cobalt chloride solution when treated with conc. HCl develops a deep blue colored solution?
options: Due to formation of $[\text{CoCl}_4]^{2+}$ having tetrahedral geometry.
Due to formation of $[\text{CoCl}_4]^{2-}$ having tetrahedral geometry.
Due to formation of $[\text{CoCl}_4]^{2-}$ having octahedral geometry.
Due to formation of $[\text{CoCl}_4]^{2+}$ having octahedral geometry.
questions.solution:
Aqueous cobalt chloride solution contains the pink-colored octahedral complex $[\text{Co}(\text{H}_2\text{O})_6]^{2+}$.

When concentrated $\text{HCl}$ is added, the chloride ions act as ligands and replace the water molecules in the coordination sphere. The reaction is given by:

$[\text{Co}(\text{H}_2\text{O})_6]^{2+} + 4\text{Cl}^- \rightleftharpoons [\text{CoCl}_4]^{2-} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$

The complex $[\text{CoCl}_4]^{2-}$ has a coordination number of $4$. Since $\text{Cl}^-$ is a weak field ligand, the complex undergoes $sp^3$ hybridization, resulting in a tetrahedral geometry.

The formation of this tetrahedral $[\text{CoCl}_4]^{2-}$ complex is responsible for the deep blue color of the solution.

Answer: Due to formation of $[\text{CoCl}_4]^{2-}$ having tetrahedral geometry.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the anionic sphere complex from following.
questions.solution:
An anionic sphere complex is a coordination compound in which the complex ion (coordination sphere) carries a net negative charge.

Triamminetrinitrocobalt(III) is $[Co(NH_3)_3(NO_2)_3]$. The net charge on the complex is $+3 + 3(0) + 3(-1) = 0$. It is a neutral complex.

Tetramminecopper(II) ion is $[Cu(NH_3)_4]^{2+}$. The net charge on the complex is $+2 + 4(0) = +2$. It is a cationic complex.

Sodium hexanitrocobaltate(III) is $Na_3[Co(NO_2)_6]$. The complex ion is $[Co(NO_2)_6]^{3-}$. The net charge on the complex sphere is $+3 + 6(-1) = -3$. It is an anionic complex.

Pentacarbonyliron(0) is $[Fe(CO)_5]$. The net charge on the complex is $0 + 5(0) = 0$. It is a neutral complex.

Therefore, Sodium hexanitrocobaltate(III) contains an anionic coordination sphere.

Answer: Sodium hexanitrocobaltate(III)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What quantity of AgCl is precipitated when one mole of Pentaamminecarbonatocobalt(III) chloride is treated with excess $\text{AgNO}_3$ (molar mass $= 143.3$ g $\text{mol}^{-1}$) solution?
questions.solution:
The formula of pentaamminecarbonatocobalt(III) chloride is $[\text{Co}(\text{NH}_3)_5(\text{CO}_3)]\text{Cl}$.

In this complex, there is one ionizable chloride ion present outside the coordination sphere.

When one mole of this complex is treated with excess $\text{AgNO}_3$ solution, it yields one mole of $\text{AgCl}$ precipitate.

$[\text{Co}(\text{NH}_3)_5(\text{CO}_3)]\text{Cl} + \text{AgNO}_3 \rightarrow [\text{Co}(\text{NH}_3)_5(\text{CO}_3)]\text{NO}_3 + \text{AgCl} \downarrow$

Mass of one mole of $\text{AgCl} = 143.3 \text{ g}$.

Therefore, $143.3 \text{ g}$ of $\text{AgCl}$ is precipitated.

Answer: 143.3 g

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify homoleptic complex from following.
questions.solution:
A homoleptic complex is a coordination compound in which the central metal atom or ion is bonded to only one type of ligand.

Bis(ethylenediamine)dithiocyanatoplatinum(IV) contains two types of ligands: ethylenediamine and thiocyanate. It is a heteroleptic complex.

Tetraamminecopper(II) ion contains only one type of ligand: ammine ($NH_3$). It is a homoleptic complex.

Triamminetrinitrocobalt(III) contains two types of ligands: ammine and nitro. It is a heteroleptic complex.

Triamminetrithiocyanatorhodium(III) contains two types of ligands: ammine and thiocyanate. It is a heteroleptic complex.

Answer: Tetraamminecopper(II) ion

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the geometry of a complex compound $[\text{CoF}_6]^{-3}$.
questions.solution:
The given complex is $[\text{CoF}_6]^{3-}$.

The central metal atom is Cobalt (Co). Let its oxidation state be $x$.

$x + 6(-1) = -3 \Rightarrow x = +3$

The electronic configuration of Co is $[\text{Ar}] 3d^7 4s^2$.

The electronic configuration of Co$^{3+}$ is $[\text{Ar}] 3d^6$.

Since F$^-$ is a weak field ligand, it does not cause pairing of electrons in the $3d$ orbitals.

The complex undergoes $sp^3d^2$ hybridization to accommodate six F$^-$ ligands.

For a coordination number of 6, the geometry of the complex is octahedral.

Answer: Octahedral

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Calculate the conductivity of $0.025$ M $\text{BaCl}_2$ solution if the molar conductivity of salt at $25^\circ\text{C}$ is $325\ \Omega^{-1}\ \text{cm}^2\ \text{mol}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
Given:
Concentration, $C = 0.025\ \text{M}$
Molar conductivity, $\Lambda_m = 325\ \Omega^{-1}\ \text{cm}^2\ \text{mol}^{-1}$

The relationship between molar conductivity and conductivity ($\kappa$) is given by:
$\Lambda_m = \dfrac{\kappa \times 1000}{C}$

Rearranging the formula to solve for $\kappa$:
$\kappa = \dfrac{\Lambda_m \times C}{1000}$

Substituting the given values:
$\kappa = \dfrac{325 \times 0.025}{1000}$

$\kappa = \dfrac{8.125}{1000}$

$\kappa = 8.125 \times 10^{-3}\ \Omega^{-1}\ \text{cm}^{-1}$

Answer: $8.125 \times 10^{-3}\ \Omega^{-1}\text{cm}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Calculate the equilibrium constant at $25\ ^\circ\text{C}$ for the cell reaction, $\text{Ni(s)} + \text{Cu}^{2+}(1\text{M}) \rightarrow \text{Ni}^{2+}(1\text{M}) + \text{Cu(s)}$ if $E^\circ_{cell}$ is $0.592\ \text{V}$.
questions.solution:
The given cell reaction is $\text{Ni(s)} + \text{Cu}^{2+}(1\text{M}) \rightarrow \text{Ni}^{2+}(1\text{M}) + \text{Cu(s)}$.

The number of electrons transferred in the reaction is $n = 2$.

The relationship between the standard cell potential and the equilibrium constant $K_c$ at $25\ ^\circ\text{C}$ is given by:

$E^\circ_{cell} = \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log K_c$

Substituting the given values:

$0.592 = \dfrac{0.0592}{2} \log K_c$

$\log K_c = \dfrac{0.592 \times 2}{0.0592}$

$\log K_c = 20$

$K_c = 10^{20} = 1.0 \times 10^{20}$

Answer: $1.0 \times 10^{20}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Calculate the time required in second to deposit 5.85 g Ni from $\text{NiCl}_2$ solution by passing 10 A current (molar mass of Ni $= 58.5\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$)
questions.solution:
The reaction for the deposition of Ni is:

$\text{Ni}^{2+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Ni}$

Number of moles of Ni deposited $= \dfrac{5.85}{58.5} = 0.1 \text{ mol}$

Since $1 \text{ mole}$ of Ni requires $2 \text{ moles}$ of electrons, the total charge required is:

$Q = 0.1 \times 2 \times 96500 = 19300 \text{ C}$

Using the formula $Q = I \times t$:

$19300 = 10 \times t$

$t = 1930 \text{ s}$

Answer: $1930$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following cell reactions is exhibited by fuel cell?
questions.solution:
The reaction $2\text{H}_{2(g)} + \text{O}_{2(g)} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$ represents the overall cell reaction of a hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell.

The reaction $\text{Cd}_{(s)} + 2\text{Ni(OH)}_{3(s)} \rightarrow \text{CdO}_{(s)} + 2\text{Ni(OH)}_{2(s)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$ occurs in a Nickel-Cadmium cell.

The reaction $\text{Pb}_{(s)} + \text{PbO}_{2(s)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_{4(aq)} \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_{4(s)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$ occurs in a lead storage battery.

The reaction $2\text{Fe}_{(s)} + \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ \rightarrow 2\text{Fe}^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$ represents the rusting of iron.

Answer: $2\text{H}_{2(g)} + \text{O}_{2(g)} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following types of cells is used for electroplating?
questions.solution:
Electroplating is a process that uses electrical energy to drive a non-spontaneous chemical reaction, specifically the deposition of a layer of metal onto a surface.

This process takes place in an electrolytic cell, where an external source of direct current is applied to cause the oxidation and reduction reactions at the electrodes.
