questions.q: Two identical inductors are connected in parallel. If the parallel combination stores $40\ \text{J}$ of magnetic field energy when a total current of $2\ \text{A}$ is passed through it, the value of the self-inductance of each coil is
questions.solution:
Let the self-inductance of each coil be $L$.

Since the two identical inductors are connected in parallel, their equivalent inductance $L_{eq}$ is given by:

$L_{eq} = \dfrac{L}{2}$

The magnetic potential energy stored in the equivalent inductor is:

$U = \dfrac{1}{2} L_{eq} I^2$

Given $U = 40\ \text{J}$ and total current $I = 2\ \text{A}$, substituting these values:

$40 = \dfrac{1}{2} \left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right) (2)^2$

$40 = \dfrac{L}{4} \times 4$

$L = 40\ \text{H}$

Answer: $40\ \text{H}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two coils have mutual inductance of $0.004\ \text{H}$. The current changes in the first coil according to the relation $i = i_0\cos\omega t$, where $i_0 = 6\ \text{A}$ and $\omega = 100\pi\ \text{rad/s}$. The maximum value of emf induced in second coil is
questions.solution:
The induced emf in the second coil is given by $e = -M \dfrac{di}{dt}$.

Given $i = i_0\cos\omega t$, differentiating with respect to time gives $\dfrac{di}{dt} = -i_0\omega\sin\omega t$.

Substituting this into the emf equation gives $e = M i_0\omega\sin\omega t$.

The maximum value of the induced emf is $e_{\text{max}} = M i_0\omega$.

Substituting the given values $M = 0.004\ \text{H}$, $i_0 = 6\ \text{A}$, and $\omega = 100\pi\ \text{rad/s}$:

$e_{\text{max}} = 0.004 \times 6 \times 100\pi = 2.4\pi\ \text{V}$.

Answer: $2.4\pi\ V$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The coefficient of coupling is $0.8$. If the mutual inductance is $8\ \text{H}$, then the values of self-inductances $L_1$ and $L_2$ of the coil are
questions.solution:
The relation between mutual inductance $M$, self-inductances $L_1$ and $L_2$, and the coefficient of coupling $k$ is given by

$M = k \sqrt{L_1 L_2}$

Substituting the given values $M = 8\ \text{H}$ and $k = 0.8$, we get

$8 = 0.8 \sqrt{L_1 L_2}$

$\sqrt{L_1 L_2} = \dfrac{8}{0.8} = 10$

Squaring both sides, we obtain

$L_1 L_2 = 100$

Checking the given options, the product of $5\ \text{H}$ and $20\ \text{H}$ is $100$.

Therefore, the values of self-inductances are $5\ \text{H}$ and $20\ \text{H}$.

Answer: $5\ \text{H}$ and $20\ \text{H}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The self inductance L of a solenoid of length $l$ and area of cross-section A, with a fixed number of turns N increases as
questions.solution:
The self-inductance of a long solenoid is given by the formula:

$L = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{l}$

where $N$ is the total number of turns, $A$ is the cross-sectional area, and $l$ is the length of the solenoid.

Since the number of turns $N$ is fixed, the self-inductance $L$ is directly proportional to the area of cross-section $A$ and inversely proportional to the length $l$.

$L \propto \dfrac{A}{l}$

Thus, to increase the self-inductance $L$, the area $A$ must increase and the length $l$ must decrease.

Answer: $l$ decreases and A increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wire of length $1\ \text{m}$ is moving at a speed of $2\ \text{m/s}$ perpendicular to its length in a homogeneous magnetic field of $0.5\ \text{T}$. If the ends of the wire are joined to a circuit of resistance $6\ \Omega$, then the rate at which work is being done to keep the wire moving at constant speed is
questions.solution:
The motional emf induced in the wire is given by

$e = Bvl$

Substituting the given values, we get

$e = 0.5 \times 2 \times 1 = 1\ \text{V}$

The rate at which work is done to keep the wire moving at a constant speed is equal to the electrical power dissipated in the circuit.

$P = \dfrac{e^2}{R}$

$P = \dfrac{1^2}{6} = \dfrac{1}{6}\ \text{W}$

Answer: $\dfrac{1}{6}\ \text{W}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Semicircular wire of radius R is rotated in a uniform magnetic field about an axis passing through its ends. If the frequency of rotation is f, the amplitude of induced e.m.f. in the wire is [B$=$magnetic field induction]
questions.solution:
The area of the semicircular region is $A = \dfrac{1}{2} \pi R^2$.

We can consider a closed loop formed by the semicircular wire and the straight segment joining its ends. Since this straight segment lies on the axis of rotation, its velocity is zero, and no motional emf is induced in it. Therefore, the emf induced in the semicircular wire is equal to the emf induced in the entire closed loop.

Assuming the magnetic field is perpendicular to the axis of rotation for maximum induced emf, the magnetic flux through the loop at any instant $t$ is given by $\Phi = B A \cos(\omega t)$, where $\omega = 2 \pi f$ is the angular frequency of rotation.

According to Faraday's law of induction, the induced emf is $\varepsilon = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = B A \omega \sin(\omega t)$.

The amplitude of the induced emf is $\varepsilon_{0} = B A \omega$.

Substituting $A = \dfrac{1}{2} \pi R^2$ and $\omega = 2 \pi f$, we get:

$\varepsilon_{0} = B \left(\dfrac{1}{2} \pi R^2\right) (2 \pi f) = \pi^2 R^2 f B$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wire of length $l$ in the form of a square loop is in a plane normal to magnetic field B. If the length of the wire is decreasing at the rate $\dfrac{dl}{dt}$, the e.m.f. induced is $e_1$. If this wire is converted into a circular loop, the e.m.f. induced in a circular loop is $e_2$. Then $\dfrac{e_1}{e_2}$ is
questions.solution:
For the square loop, the side length is $a = \dfrac{l}{4}$.

Area of the square loop is $A_1 = a^2 = \dfrac{l^2}{16}$.

The induced e.m.f. $e_1$ is given by $e_1 = \left| \dfrac{d\Phi_1}{dt} \right| = B \dfrac{dA_1}{dt} = B \dfrac{d}{dt} \left( \dfrac{l^2}{16} \right) = \dfrac{Bl}{8} \left| \dfrac{dl}{dt} \right|$.

For the circular loop, the circumference is $l$, so the radius is $r = \dfrac{l}{2\pi}$.

Area of the circular loop is $A_2 = \pi r^2 = \pi \left( \dfrac{l}{2\pi} \right)^2 = \dfrac{l^2}{4\pi}$.

The induced e.m.f. $e_2$ is given by $e_2 = \left| \dfrac{d\Phi_2}{dt} \right| = B \dfrac{dA_2}{dt} = B \dfrac{d}{dt} \left( \dfrac{l^2}{4\pi} \right) = \dfrac{Bl}{2\pi} \left| \dfrac{dl}{dt} \right|$.

Taking the ratio of the two induced e.m.f.s:

$\dfrac{e_1}{e_2} = \dfrac{\dfrac{Bl}{8} \left| \dfrac{dl}{dt} \right|}{\dfrac{Bl}{2\pi} \left| \dfrac{dl}{dt} \right|} = \dfrac{2\pi}{8} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$.

Answer: $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A very small circular loop of radius 'a' is initially (at $t = 0$) coplanar and concentric with a much larger fixed circular loop of radius 'b'. A constant current I flows in the larger loop. The smaller loop is rotated with a constant angular speed $\omega$ about the common diameter. The emf induced in the smaller loop as a function of time t is
($\mu_0$ = permeability of free space)
questions.solution:
The magnetic field produced by the larger loop at its centre is given by

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2b}$

Since the smaller loop is very small ($a \ll b$), the magnetic field over its area can be assumed to be uniform and equal to $B$.

The magnetic flux linked with the smaller loop at any time $t$ is

$\Phi = \vec{B} \cdot \vec{A} = B A \cos(\omega t)$

Substituting $A = \pi a^2$ and $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2b}$, we get

$\Phi = \left(\dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2b}\right) (\pi a^2) \cos(\omega t)$

According to Faraday's law of induction, the induced emf is

$e = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = -\dfrac{d}{dt} \left[ \left(\dfrac{\mu_0 I \pi a^2}{2b}\right) \cos(\omega t) \right]$

$e = \left(\dfrac{\pi a^2 \mu_0 I}{2b}\right) \omega \sin(\omega t)$

Answer: $\left(\dfrac{\pi a^2\mu_0 I}{2b}\right)\omega\sin\omega t$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A thin semicircular conducting ring of radius R is falling with its plane vertical in a horizontal magnetic induction B. At the position MNQ the speed of ring is V and emf developed across the ring is
options: $2 RBV$ and Q is at higher potential
$RBV$ and Q is at higher potential
$\dfrac{BV\pi R^2}{2}$ and M is at higher potential
questions.solution:
The induced motional emf in an arbitrarily shaped moving conductor in a uniform magnetic field is equivalent to the emf induced in a straight conductor connecting its two ends.

The effective length of the semicircular ring is the straight line distance between its ends M and Q.

Effective length $l = 2R$

The magnitude of the motional emf developed is given by $\varepsilon = Bvl$

Substituting the values, we get $\varepsilon = B \cdot V \cdot 2R = 2RBV$

To determine the polarity, we consider the magnetic force on a positive charge carrier, given by $\vec{F} = q(\vec{V} \times \vec{B})$

Since the velocity vector $\vec{V}$ is downwards and the magnetic field vector $\vec{B}$ is into the plane of the paper, the cross product $\vec{V} \times \vec{B}$ points towards the right.

Positive charges are pushed towards end Q, making Q at a higher potential than end M.

Answer: $2 RBV$ and Q is at higher potential

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A magnetic field induction is changing in magnitude at a constant rate $\dfrac{dB}{dt}$. The plane of a circular loop of radius $r$ of copper wire (mass $m$ and radius $a$) is placed perpendicular to the field. The induced current in the loop is {$\rho$ = specific resistance, $\delta$ = density of copper}
questions.solution:
The induced electromotive force in the circular loop is given by Faraday's law of induction:

$E = A \dfrac{dB}{dt} = \pi r^2 \dfrac{dB}{dt}$

The resistance of the copper wire forming the loop is:

$R = \rho \dfrac{l}{A'} = \rho \dfrac{2\pi r}{\pi a^2}$

The induced current in the loop is:

$I = \dfrac{E}{R} = \dfrac{\pi r^2 \dfrac{dB}{dt}}{\rho \dfrac{2\pi r}{\pi a^2}} = \dfrac{\pi r a^2}{2\rho} \dfrac{dB}{dt}$

The mass of the copper wire is the product of its volume and density:

$m = (\pi a^2)(2\pi r)\delta = 2\pi^2 a^2 r \delta$

Rearranging the above expression yields:

$\pi r a^2 = \dfrac{m}{2\pi \delta}$

Substituting this into the expression for the induced current, we get:

$I = \dfrac{1}{2\rho} \left( \dfrac{m}{2\pi \delta} \right) \dfrac{dB}{dt} = \dfrac{m}{4\pi \rho \delta} \dfrac{dB}{dt}$

Answer: $\dfrac{m}{4\pi\rho\delta}\dfrac{dB}{dt}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When four inductors of the same inductances L are connected in series, and I is the current passing through the circuit. The energy stored in the circuit is
questions.solution:
The equivalent inductance of four inductors connected in series is given by $L_{eq} = L + L + L + L = 4L$.

The energy stored in the circuit is given by $U = \dfrac{1}{2} L_{eq} I^2$.

Substituting the value of $L_{eq}$, we get $U = \dfrac{1}{2} (4L) I^2 = 2LI^2$.

Answer: $2LI^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The self-inductance L of a toroid having circular cross-section of radius $r$ and major radius $R$ is ($r \ll R$)
questions.solution:
The magnetic field inside a toroid of major radius $R$ is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I}{2 \pi R}$, assuming $r \ll R$ so that the field is approximately uniform across the cross-section.

The cross-sectional area of the toroid is $A = \pi r^2$.

The magnetic flux through a single turn is $\phi = B A = \left(\dfrac{\mu_0 N I}{2 \pi R}\right) (\pi r^2) = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I r^2}{2 R}$.

The total flux linkage for $N$ turns is $\Phi = N \phi = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 I r^2}{2 R}$.

The self-inductance is $L = \dfrac{\Phi}{I} = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 r^2}{2 R}$.

Answer: $\dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 r^2}{2R}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an a.c. generator, a rectangular coil of $1000$ turns each having area $\dfrac{10^{-2}}{\pi}\ \text{m}^2$ is rotated at $180$ r.p.m. about an axis perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of magnitude $2$ T. The maximum value of e.m.f. produced will be
questions.solution:
The maximum e.m.f. produced in an a.c. generator is given by $E_0 = NAB\omega$

Given:
$N = 1000$
$A = \dfrac{10^{-2}}{\pi}\ \text{m}^2$
$B = 2\ \text{T}$
Frequency $f = 180\ \text{r.p.m.} = \dfrac{180}{60}\ \text{Hz} = 3\ \text{Hz}$
Angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 3 = 6\pi\ \text{rad/s}$

Substituting the values:
$E_0 = 1000 \times \dfrac{10^{-2}}{\pi} \times 2 \times 6\pi$

$E_0 = 10 \times 12 = 120\ \text{V}$

Answer: $120$ V

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two conducting circular loops of radii $R_1$ and $R_2$ are placed in same plane with their centers coinciding. If $R_1 > R_2$ and current I is flowing through the circular loop of radius $R_1$, then mutual inductance between them is proportional to
questions.solution:
Magnetic field at the center of the larger loop of radius $R_1$ carrying current $I$ is given by

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 R_1}$

Since $R_1 > R_2$, the magnetic field can be considered approximately uniform over the entire area of the smaller loop.

Magnetic flux linked with the smaller loop of radius $R_2$ is

$\Phi = B \times A_2 = \left(\dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 R_1}\right) (\pi R_2^2)$

The mutual inductance $M$ is given by

$M = \dfrac{\Phi}{I} = \dfrac{\mu_0 \pi R_2^2}{2 R_1}$

Therefore, $M \propto \dfrac{R_2^2}{R_1}$

Answer: $\dfrac{R_2^2}{R_1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting rod AB is in contact with metal rails AC and BD, which are $0.25$ m apart in a uniform magnetic field of induction $0.4$ T acting perpendicular to the plane of the figure. Ends C and D are connected through a $5\ \Omega$ resistor. If the rod AB moves to right with a velocity of $5$ m/s, then the magnitude and direction of the current through rod AB is
questions.solution:
The motional emf induced in the moving rod is given by:
$E = Bvl$

Substituting the given values ($B = 0.4\text{ T}$, $v = 5\text{ m/s}$, $l = 0.25\text{ m}$):
$E = 0.4 \times 5 \times 0.25 = 0.5\text{ V}$

The magnitude of the induced current is:
$I = \dfrac{E}{R}$
$I = \dfrac{0.5}{5} = 0.1\text{ A}$

The direction of the induced current can be determined using Fleming's Right-Hand Rule or the Lorentz force on positive charges ($\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$).
With velocity $\vec{v}$ towards the right and magnetic field $\vec{B}$ into the plane of the paper, the force on positive charges is directed upwards.
Therefore, the induced current in the rod AB flows from B to A.

Answer: $0.1$ A from B to A

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A square loop (side $a$) rotates in a uniform magnetic field $B$ perpendicular to the rotation axis. What is the maximum induced EMF ($e_{\text{max}}$) and the first angle $\theta$ (between magnetic field $B$ and the loop normal) where it occurs?
questions.solution:
The magnetic flux through the loop at any instant is given by $\Phi = \vec{B} \cdot \vec{A} = B A \cos\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between the magnetic field and the normal to the loop area.

Since the loop rotates with an angular velocity $\omega$, we have $\theta = \omega t$.

The area of the square loop is $A = a^2$.

The induced EMF is given by Faraday's law of induction:

$e = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = -\dfrac{d}{dt}(B a^2 \cos\theta) = B a^2 \omega \sin\theta$

The maximum induced EMF occurs when $\sin\theta = 1$.

The first angle where this occurs is $\theta = 90^\circ$.

The maximum induced EMF is $e_{\text{max}} = B a^2 \omega$.

Answer: $Ba^2\omega$; $\theta = 90^\circ$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Average e.m.f. of 2.4 V is induced in a coil when a current in it is changed from 10 A in one direction to 10 A in opposite direction in 0.4 second. The self inductance of the coil is
questions.solution:
Given $e = 2.4 \text{ V}$, $I_1 = 10 \text{ A}$, $I_2 = -10 \text{ A}$, and $\Delta t = 0.4 \text{ s}$.

The magnitude of the average induced e.m.f. is given by:

$|e| = L \dfrac{|\Delta I|}{\Delta t}$

Substituting the given values:

$2.4 = L \dfrac{|-10 - 10|}{0.4}$

$2.4 = L \dfrac{20}{0.4}$

$2.4 = 50 L$

$L = \dfrac{2.4}{50} = 0.048 \text{ H}$

$L = 48 \text{ mH}$

Answer: $48 \text{ mH}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metal rod of length '$l$' rotates about one of its ends in a plane perpendicular to a magnetic field of induction 'B'. If the emf induced between the ends of the rod is 'e', then the number of revolutions made by the rod per second is
questions.solution:
The induced emf $e$ across the ends of a metal rod of length $l$ rotating with angular velocity $\omega$ in a perpendicular magnetic field $B$ is given by:

$e = \dfrac{1}{2} B \omega l^2$

The angular velocity $\omega$ is related to the frequency $f$ (number of revolutions per second) by:

$\omega = 2 \pi f$

Substituting the value of $\omega$ in the expression for emf:

$e = \dfrac{1}{2} B (2 \pi f) l^2$

$e = B \pi f l^2$

Rearranging for $f$, we get:

$f = \dfrac{e}{B \pi l^2}$

Answer: $\dfrac{e}{B\pi l^2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two coils P and S have a mutual inductance of $5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{H}$. If the current in the primary (P) is $I = 10\sin(100\pi t)$, then the maximum value of e.m.f. induced in S is (in volt)
questions.solution:
The induced e.m.f. in the secondary coil is given by:

$e = -M \dfrac{dI}{dt}$

Given $M = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{H}$ and $I = 10\sin(100\pi t)$.

Differentiating the current with respect to time:

$\dfrac{dI}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt} (10\sin(100\pi t)) = 1000\pi \cos(100\pi t)$

Substituting the values into the e.m.f. equation:

$e = -(5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1000\pi \cos(100\pi t)$

$e = -5\pi \cos(100\pi t)$

The maximum value of the induced e.m.f. corresponds to the maximum magnitude of the cosine function, which is $1$.

$e_{max} = 5\pi\ \text{V}$

Answer: $5\pi$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnetic flux through a coil of resistance $R$ changes by an amount $\Delta\Phi$ in the time $\Delta t$. The total quantity of electric charge ($Q$) induced is
questions.solution:
According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the magnitude of induced emf is given by

$|e| = \dfrac{\Delta\Phi}{\Delta t}$

The induced current in the coil of resistance $R$ is

$I = \dfrac{|e|}{R} = \dfrac{\Delta\Phi}{R \Delta t}$

The total quantity of electric charge $Q$ induced in time $\Delta t$ is

$Q = I \Delta t = \left(\dfrac{\Delta\Phi}{R \Delta t}\right) \Delta t = \dfrac{\Delta\Phi}{R}$

Answer: $Q = \dfrac{\Delta\Phi}{R}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Identify the CORRECT statement
options: The direction of eddy currents can be determined by the Left-Hand Rule.
Eddy currents always perform useful work and increase the efficiency of all electrical devices.
Eddy currents circulate in planes perpendicular to the magnetic field that induces them.
Eddy currents are present only in non-magnetic materials like aluminum
questions.solution:
Eddy currents are induced in bulk pieces of conductors when they are subjected to a changing magnetic flux.

According to Faraday's law of induction, the induced electric field and the resulting eddy currents circulate in closed loops within the conductor, in planes perpendicular to the changing magnetic field.

The direction of eddy currents is determined by Lenz's law or Fleming's Right-Hand Rule, not the Left-Hand Rule.

Eddy currents often cause undesirable heating and energy loss in electrical devices like transformers, reducing their efficiency.

Eddy currents can be induced in any conducting material, whether magnetic (like iron) or non-magnetic (like aluminum).

Answer: Eddy currents circulate in planes perpendicular to the magnetic field that induces them.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The north pole of the magnet is brought near a metallic ring as shown in figure. The direction of induced current in the ring (if looked from upside) will be
questions.solution:
As the north pole of the magnet is brought near the metallic ring, the downward magnetic flux linked with the ring increases.

According to Lenz's law, the induced current in the ring will flow in a direction such that it opposes the change in magnetic flux. To oppose the increasing downward flux, the induced current must produce an upward magnetic field.

Using the right-hand grip rule, an upward magnetic field is generated when the current flows in an anti-clockwise direction. Therefore, when viewed from above, the direction of the induced current in the ring will be anti-clockwise.

Answer: anti-clockwise.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A thin semicircular conducting ring (PQR) of radius r is falling with its plane vertical in a horizontal magnetic field B as shown in the figure. The potential difference developed across the ring when its speed is v, is
$\{\sin(90)^\circ=1\}$
options: $\pi rBv$ and terminal R is at higher potential
$\dfrac{Bv\pi r^{2}}{2}$ and terminal P is at higher potential
$2rBv$ and terminal R is at higher potential
questions.solution:
The motional emf induced in a conductor moving in a uniform magnetic field is given by $\varepsilon = (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot \vec{l}$, where $\vec{l}$ is the vector connecting the ends of the conductor.

For the semicircular ring PQR, the effective length vector $\vec{l}$ is the straight line from P to R, which has a magnitude equal to the diameter of the semicircle.

$l_{\text{eff}} = 2r$

Since $\vec{v}$, $\vec{B}$, and $\vec{l}$ are mutually perpendicular, the magnitude of the induced motional emf is:

$\varepsilon = B v l_{\text{eff}} = 2rBv$

The polarity of the induced emf is determined by the direction of the magnetic force on the positive charges, given by $\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$.

The velocity $\vec{v}$ is directed downwards and the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is directed into the plane of the paper.

By the right-hand rule, the cross product $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ points towards the right.

This means positive charges are pushed towards terminal R, making it at a higher potential than terminal P.

Therefore, the potential difference developed across the ring is $2rBv$ with terminal R at a higher potential.

Answer: $2rBv$ and terminal R is at higher potential

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metallic rod of length $'l'$ is tied to a string of length $'2l'$ and made to rotate with angular speed $'2\omega'$ on a horizontal table with one end of the string fixed. If there exists a vertical magnetic field 'B' in the region, the emf $'e'$ induced across the ends of rod is
questions.solution:
Let the fixed end of the string be the origin. The rod is situated between the distances $r_1 = 2l$ and $r_2 = 2l + l = 3l$ from the fixed end.

Consider a small element of the rod of length $dx$ at a distance $x$ from the fixed end. The velocity of this element is $v = x(2\omega)$.

The motional emf induced in this small element is $de = Bv\,dx = B(x \cdot 2\omega)\,dx$.

The total induced emf across the ends of the rod is obtained by integrating $de$ from $x = 2l$ to $x = 3l$:

$e = \int_{2l}^{3l} 2B\omega x\,dx$

$e = 2B\omega \left[ \dfrac{x^2}{2} \right]_{2l}^{3l}$

$e = B\omega ((3l)^2 - (2l)^2)$

$e = B\omega (9l^2 - 4l^2)$

$e = 5B\omega l^2$

Answer: $5\,B\omega\,l^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The current in a primary coil of a step-down transformer having $N_p$ turns is given by $i = i_0 \cos\omega t$. The secondary coil of having $N_s$ turns is wound over the primary coil. The emf induced in the secondary is
questions.solution:
The current in the primary coil is given by $i = i_0 \cos\omega t$.

The magnetic flux linked with the secondary coil is $\phi_s = M i$, where $M$ is the mutual inductance between the coils.

According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the induced emf in the secondary coil is given by:

$e = -\dfrac{d\phi_s}{dt}$

$e = -M \dfrac{di}{dt}$

Differentiating the expression for current with respect to time $t$:

$\dfrac{di}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt}(i_0 \cos\omega t) = -i_0 \omega \sin\omega t$

Substituting this back into the equation for induced emf:

$e = -M (-i_0 \omega \sin\omega t)$

$e = \omega M i_0 \sin\omega t$

Answer: $\omega M i_0 \sin\omega t$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The variation of induced emf (E) with time (t) in a coil if a short bar magnet is moved along its axis with a constant velocity is best represented as $\{$North pole is situated at left end of the bar magnet and South pole is situated at right end of the bar magnet$\}$
questions.solution:
As the short bar magnet approaches the solenoid, the magnetic flux linked with the coil increases. According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, an emf is induced. By Lenz's law, this induced emf opposes the increase in flux, resulting in an emf of a particular sign (e.g., negative).

When the short magnet is completely inside the long solenoid and moving with constant velocity, the total magnetic flux linked with the solenoid remains constant. Since the rate of change of flux is zero ($\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = 0$), the induced emf becomes zero during this time interval.

As the magnet exits the other end of the solenoid, the magnetic flux linked with the coil decreases. This induces an emf in the opposite direction (positive) to oppose the decrease in flux.

Therefore, the induced emf first shows a pulse in one direction, then becomes zero for a while, and finally shows a pulse in the opposite direction. Only graph (B) correctly represents this variation.

Answer: B

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two coils P and Q are kept near each other. When no current flows through coil P and currrent in coil Q increases at the rate of 10 A/s, the e.m.f. in coil P is 12 mV. When coil Q carries no current and current 1.5 A flows through coil P, the magnetic flux linked with the coil Q is
questions.solution:
The induced e.m.f. in coil P due to the rate of change of current in coil Q is given by

$e_P = M \dfrac{di_Q}{dt}$

Substituting the given values,

$12 \times 10^{-3} = M \times 10$

$M = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ H}$

The magnetic flux linked with coil Q when current $i_P$ flows through coil P is

$\phi_Q = M i_P$

$\phi_Q = (1.2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1.5$

$\phi_Q = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb} = 1.8 \text{ mWb}$

Answer: $1.8 \text{ mWb}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If '$N$' is the number of turns in a circular coil, the value of its self-inductance varies as
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of a circular coil of radius $R$ having $N$ turns carrying current $I$ is given by

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I}{2R}$

The magnetic flux linked with a single turn of the coil is

$\phi_1 = B A = \left(\dfrac{\mu_0 N I}{2R}\right) (\pi R^2) = \dfrac{\mu_0 \pi N I R}{2}$

The total magnetic flux linked with the entire coil of $N$ turns is

$\phi = N \phi_1 = N \left(\dfrac{\mu_0 \pi N I R}{2}\right) = \dfrac{\mu_0 \pi N^2 I R}{2}$

The self-inductance $L$ of the coil is defined by $\phi = L I$. Therefore,

$L = \dfrac{\phi}{I} = \dfrac{\mu_0 \pi N^2 R}{2}$

From this expression, it is clear that the self-inductance $L$ is directly proportional to the square of the number of turns.

$L \propto N^2$

Answer: $N^{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel-plate capacitor of $50\ \mu\text{F}$ contains a dielectric slab of constant ($k$) with the same dimensions. If a time-varying potential difference is applied at a rate of $50\ \text{V/s}$. What is the displacement current in the capacitor?
questions.solution:
The displacement current $I_d$ in a capacitor is equal to the conduction current $I_c$ flowing into it.

$I_d = I_c = \dfrac{dQ}{dt}$

Since $Q = CV$, we have:

$I_d = C \dfrac{dV}{dt}$

Given:
$C = 50\ \mu\text{F} = 50 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{F}$
$\dfrac{dV}{dt} = 50\ \text{V/s}$

Substituting the values:

$I_d = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 50$

$I_d = 2500 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A}$

$I_d = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A} = 2.5\ \text{mA}$

Answer: $2.5\ \text{mA}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The electric field at a point in a certain region of free space due to a charge Q is 0. The electric potential at that point is
options: directly proportional to the charge Q.
questions.solution:
The electric field due to a point charge $Q$ at a distance $r$ is given by $E = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0}\dfrac{Q}{r^2}$.

Given that the electric field $E = 0$, this implies that the distance $r \to \infty$ (assuming $Q \neq 0$).

The electric potential due to the charge $Q$ at a distance $r$ is given by $V = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0}\dfrac{Q}{r}$.

As $r \to \infty$, the electric potential $V \to 0$.

Therefore, the electric potential at that point is zero.

Answer: zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A closed surface encloses $n$ electric dipoles. The net electric flux through the closed surface is
($\epsilon_0$ = permittivity of free space, q = charge)
questions.solution:
An electric dipole consists of two equal and opposite charges, $+q$ and $-q$.

The net charge of a single electric dipole is $+q - q = 0$.

For $n$ electric dipoles enclosed in a closed surface, the total enclosed charge is $Q_{enclosed} = n \times 0 = 0$.

According to Gauss's law, the net electric flux through a closed surface is given by $\Phi = \dfrac{Q_{enclosed}}{\epsilon_0}$.

Substituting $Q_{enclosed} = 0$, we get $\Phi = 0$.

Answer: $0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A hollow metal sphere of radius $5\ \text{cm}$ is charged such that the potential on its surface is $10\ \text{V}$. The electric field at its centre is
questions.solution:
For a hollow metal sphere, the charge resides entirely on its outer surface.

The electric field inside a charged conductor is zero everywhere.

Therefore, the electric field at the centre of the sphere is $0\ \text{V/m}$.

Answer: $0\ \text{V/m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Four infinitely large planar charged sheets are placed parallel to each other as shown in Figure 1. Find the magnitude of electric field at point P.
questions.solution:
The electric field due to an infinitely large planar charged sheet is given by $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{2\epsilon_0}$, and it is independent of the distance from the sheet.

Let the rightward direction be positive.

Point P is located to the right of sheets 1, 2, and 3, and to the left of sheet 4.

Electric field at P due to sheet 1: $E_1 = \dfrac{3\sigma}{2\epsilon_0}$ (towards right)

Electric field at P due to sheet 2: $E_2 = \dfrac{\sigma}{2\epsilon_0}$ (towards right)

Electric field at P due to sheet 3: $E_3 = -\dfrac{2\sigma}{2\epsilon_0}$ (towards left, as it is negatively charged)

Electric field at P due to sheet 4: $E_4 = -\dfrac{4\sigma}{2\epsilon_0}$ (towards left, as it is positively charged and P is to its left)

Net electric field at P is the vector sum of these fields:

$E_{net} = E_1 + E_2 + E_3 + E_4$

$E_{net} = \dfrac{3\sigma}{2\epsilon_0} + \dfrac{\sigma}{2\epsilon_0} - \dfrac{2\sigma}{2\epsilon_0} - \dfrac{4\sigma}{2\epsilon_0}$

$E_{net} = \dfrac{3\sigma + \sigma - 2\sigma - 4\sigma}{2\epsilon_0} = \dfrac{-2\sigma}{2\epsilon_0} = -\dfrac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}$

The magnitude of the electric field at point P is $\dfrac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}$.

Answer: $\dfrac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The electric potential 'V' as a function of distance x (in metre) is $V = 4x^2 + 6x - 8$ volt. Electric field at $x = 2$ m is
questions.solution:
The relation between electric field $E$ and electric potential $V$ is given by $E = -\dfrac{dV}{dx}$.

Given $V = 4x^2 + 6x - 8$.

Differentiating with respect to $x$:

$\dfrac{dV}{dx} = \dfrac{d}{dx}(4x^2 + 6x - 8) = 8x + 6$

Therefore, $E = -(8x + 6)$.

At $x = 2$ m:

$E = -(8(2) + 6) = -(16 + 6) = -22\ \text{V/m}$.

Answer: $-22\ \text{V/m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform electric field is given as $\vec{E} = 4 \times 10^4\,\hat{j}\ \text{N/C}$. The flux of this field through a square of side $10\ \text{cm}$ whose plane is parallel to the Y-Z plane is
questions.solution:
The electric field is given as $\vec{E} = 4 \times 10^4\,\hat{j}\ \text{N/C}$.

The area of the square is $A = (10\ \text{cm})^2 = 0.01\ \text{m}^2$.

Since the plane of the square is parallel to the Y-Z plane, its area vector is perpendicular to the Y-Z plane, which means it is directed along the X-axis.

$\vec{A} = 0.01\,\hat{i}\ \text{m}^2$

The electric flux $\Phi$ through the square is given by the dot product of the electric field and the area vector:

$\Phi = \vec{E} \cdot \vec{A}$

$\Phi = (4 \times 10^4\,\hat{j}) \cdot (0.01\,\hat{i})$

Since $\hat{j} \cdot \hat{i} = 0$, we get:

$\Phi = 0$

Answer: zero

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assume that the electric field $\vec{E} = 30 x^2 \hat{i}$ exists in space. Then the potential difference $V_A - V_O$, where '$V_O$' is the potential at the origin and '$V_A$' is the potential at $x = 2$ m, is
questions.solution:
The relation between electric potential and electric field is given by $dV = -\vec{E} \cdot d\vec{r}$.

Substituting the given electric field $\vec{E} = 30x^2 \hat{i}$ and $d\vec{r} = dx \hat{i}$:

$dV = -(30x^2 \hat{i}) \cdot (dx \hat{i}) = -30x^2 dx$

Integrating both sides from the origin ($x = 0$) to point A ($x = 2$):

$\int_{V_O}^{V_A} dV = \int_{0}^{2} -30x^2 dx$

$V_A - V_O = -30 \left[ \dfrac{x^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{2}$

$V_A - V_O = -10 (2^3 - 0^3)$

$V_A - V_O = -10 \times 8 = -80$ V

Answer: $-80$ V

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two point charges $+8q$ and $-2q$ are located at $x=0$ and $x=L$ respectively. What is the location of a point on the x-axis from the origin, at which the net electric field due to these two point charges is zero?
questions.solution:
Let the point where the net electric field is zero be at a distance $x$ from the origin on the x-axis.

Since the charges have opposite signs, the null point must lie outside the region between the charges and closer to the charge with the smaller magnitude. Thus, the point lies at $x > L$.

Equating the magnitudes of the electric fields due to both charges at this point:

$\dfrac{k(8q)}{x^2} = \dfrac{k(2q)}{(x-L)^2}$

$\dfrac{4}{x^2} = \dfrac{1}{(x-L)^2}$

Taking the square root on both sides:

$\dfrac{2}{x} = \dfrac{1}{x-L}$

$2x - 2L = x$

$x = 2L$

Answer: $2L$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three point charges $q_1 = 1\ \mu\text{C}$, $q_2 = -2\ \mu\text{C}$, and $q_3 = 3\ \mu\text{C}$ are placed at $(1, 0, 0)$, $(0, 2, 0)$ and $(0, 0, 3)$ respectively. The electric potential at origin is $\left\{\dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} = 9 \times 10^9\ \text{SI unit}\right\}$
questions.solution:
The electric potential at the origin is given by the superposition principle:

$V = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \left( \dfrac{q_1}{r_1} + \dfrac{q_2}{r_2} + \dfrac{q_3}{r_3} \right)$

The distances of the charges from the origin are:

$r_1 = \sqrt{1^2 + 0^2 + 0^2} = 1\ \text{m}$

$r_2 = \sqrt{0^2 + 2^2 + 0^2} = 2\ \text{m}$

$r_3 = \sqrt{0^2 + 0^2 + 3^2} = 3\ \text{m}$

Substituting the values of charges and distances:

$V = 9 \times 10^9 \left( \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{1} + \dfrac{-2 \times 10^{-6}}{2} + \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-6}}{3} \right)$

$V = 9 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-6} (1 - 1 + 1)$

$V = 9 \times 10^3\ \text{V}$

Answer: $9 \times 10^3\ \text{V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three electric charges $2q$, $2q$, $-4q$ are placed at the corners of equilateral triangle of side $'x'$. The magnitude of electric dipole moment of the system is
questions.solution:
The system of charges can be considered as two dipoles by splitting the $-4q$ charge into two charges of $-2q$ each.

One dipole is formed by $-2q$ and $2q$ along one side of the equilateral triangle, having a dipole moment $p_1 = (2q)x = 2qx$.

The second dipole is formed by the other $-2q$ and $2q$ along the adjacent side, having a dipole moment $p_2 = (2q)x = 2qx$.

The angle between the two dipole moment vectors is $60^{\circ}$, which is the interior angle of the equilateral triangle.

The magnitude of the resultant dipole moment is given by:

$p_{net} = \sqrt{p_1^2 + p_2^2 + 2p_1p_2\cos 60^{\circ}}$

Substituting $p_1 = p_2 = 2qx$ and $\cos 60^{\circ} = \dfrac{1}{2}$:

$p_{net} = \sqrt{(2qx)^2 + (2qx)^2 + 2(2qx)(2qx)\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)}$

$p_{net} = \sqrt{4q^2x^2 + 4q^2x^2 + 4q^2x^2}$

$p_{net} = \sqrt{12q^2x^2} = 2\sqrt{3}qx$

Answer: $2\sqrt{3}qx$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three charges Q, $+q$ and $+q$ are placed at the vertices of the right angle triangle (isosceles triangle) as shown. The net electrostatic energy of the configuration is zero, if Q is equal to [a $=$ length of two equal sides of a triangle]
questions.solution:
The net electrostatic potential energy of a system of charges is the sum of the potential energies of all pairs of charges.

Let the charges be placed at the vertices of the right-angled isosceles triangle. From the given figure, the charges are $Q$, $+q$, and $+q$.
The distances between the pairs of charges are:
1. Distance between $Q$ and $+q$ (vertical side) $= a$
2. Distance between $+q$ and $+q$ (horizontal side) $= a$
3. Distance between $Q$ and $+q$ (hypotenuse) $= \sqrt{a^2 + a^2} = a\sqrt{2}$

The total electrostatic potential energy $U$ of the system is given by:
$U = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \left( \dfrac{Q \cdot q}{a} + \dfrac{q \cdot q}{a} + \dfrac{Q \cdot q}{a\sqrt{2}} \right)$

Given that the net electrostatic energy of the configuration is zero, we have $U = 0$:
$\dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \left( \dfrac{Qq}{a} + \dfrac{q^2}{a} + \dfrac{Qq}{a\sqrt{2}} \right) = 0$

Dividing the entire equation by $\dfrac{q}{4\pi\epsilon_0 a}$ (assuming $q \neq 0$), we get:
$Q + q + \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{2}} = 0$

Grouping the terms with $Q$:
$Q \left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \right) + q = 0$

$Q \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{2} + 1}{\sqrt{2}} \right) = -q$

$Q = \dfrac{-\sqrt{2}q}{\sqrt{2} + 1}$

Answer: $\dfrac{-\sqrt{2}q}{1+\sqrt{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three metallic spherical shells (not concentric) of radii R, 2R and 4R are given charges $Q_1$, $Q_2$ and $Q_3$ respectively. It is found that the surface charge densities on the outer surfaces of the shells are equal. Then, the ratio of the charges $Q_1:Q_2:Q_3$, is
questions.solution:
For isolated (not concentric) metallic spherical shells, the charge given to each shell resides entirely on its outer surface.

The surface charge density $\sigma$ of a spherical shell of radius $r$ and charge $Q$ is given by:
$\sigma = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi r^2}$

Given that the surface charge densities on the outer surfaces of the three shells are equal:
$\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma_3$

Substituting the values of charges and radii for each shell:
$\dfrac{Q_1}{4\pi R^2} = \dfrac{Q_2}{4\pi (2R)^2} = \dfrac{Q_3}{4\pi (4R)^2}$

$\dfrac{Q_1}{R^2} = \dfrac{Q_2}{4R^2} = \dfrac{Q_3}{16R^2}$

Multiplying by $R^2$, we get the ratio of the charges:
$Q_1 : Q_2 : Q_3 = 1 : 4 : 16$

Answer: $1:4:16$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three charges each of $+4\,\mu\text{C}$ are placed at the corners A, B, C of a square ABCD of side $1$ m. The electric field at the centre of the square is
questions.solution:
Let the center of the square be O. The distance from each corner to the center is $r = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ m.

The electric field at O due to the charge at A is equal and opposite to the electric field at O due to the charge at C. Hence, they cancel each other out.

The net electric field at O is solely due to the charge at B. Since the charge at B is positive, the electric field points away from B, i.e., towards D.

The magnitude of the electric field is given by $E = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \dfrac{q}{r^2}$.

Substituting the values, we get $E = \dfrac{9 \times 10^9 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}{(1/\sqrt{2})^2}$.

$E = \dfrac{36 \times 10^3}{1/2} = 72 \times 10^3 = 7.2 \times 10^4$ N/C.

The direction is towards point D.

Answer: $7.2 \times 10^4$ N/C towards point D

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Which of the following statements is NOT TRUE for polar molecule?
a. In polar molecule the centre of mass of positive charges coincide with the centre of mass of negative charges.
b. They have permanent dipole moment.
c. They have asymmetric shape.
d. HCL, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ are the examples of polar molecules
questions.solution:
In a polar molecule, the centre of positive charge does not coincide with the centre of negative charge. This separation of charges is what gives the molecule a permanent electric dipole moment.

Therefore, the statement that the centre of mass of positive charges coincides with the centre of mass of negative charges is incorrect for polar molecules. This property belongs to non-polar molecules.

Statements b, c, and d correctly describe the properties and examples of polar molecules.

Answer: a

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two point charges $8q$ and $2q$ are located at $x=0$ and $x=L$ respectively. The location of a point on the X-axis from origin, at which the net electric field is zero, is $x$. Value of $x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let the point where the net electric field is zero be at a distance $x$ from the origin.

Since both charges are positive, the null point must lie between them. The distance of this point from the charge $8q$ is $x$ and from the charge $2q$ is $L - x$.

Equating the magnitudes of the electric fields produced by both charges at this point:

$\dfrac{k(8q)}{x^2} = \dfrac{k(2q)}{(L - x)^2}$

$\dfrac{4}{x^2} = \dfrac{1}{(L - x)^2}$

Taking the square root on both sides:

$\dfrac{2}{x} = \dfrac{1}{L - x}$

$2(L - x) = x$

$2L - 2x = x$

$3x = 2L$

$x = \dfrac{2L}{3}$

Answer: $\dfrac{2L}{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge Q is distributed over the two concentric hollow spheres of radii $r$ and $R$ ($R > r$) such the surface densities are equal. The potential at the common centre is {$K = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\epsilon_0}$, $\epsilon_0$ = permittivity of free space}
questions.solution:
Let $q_1$ and $q_2$ be the charges on the inner and outer spheres respectively.

Given $q_1 + q_2 = Q$

Since the surface charge densities are equal, $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma$

$\dfrac{q_1}{4\pi r^2} = \dfrac{q_2}{4\pi R^2} = \sigma$

$q_1 = \sigma 4\pi r^2$ and $q_2 = \sigma 4\pi R^2$

Total charge $Q = \sigma 4\pi (r^2 + R^2) \Rightarrow \sigma = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi (r^2 + R^2)}$

The potential at the common centre is the sum of the potentials due to both spheres:

$V = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \dfrac{q_1}{r} + \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \dfrac{q_2}{R}$

$V = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \left( \dfrac{\sigma 4\pi r^2}{r} + \dfrac{\sigma 4\pi R^2}{R} \right)$

$V = \dfrac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0} (r + R)$

Substituting the value of $\sigma$:

$V = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\epsilon_0 (r^2 + R^2)} (r + R)$

Given $K = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\epsilon_0}$, we get:

$V = \dfrac{K(r+R)}{R^2+r^2}$

Answer: $\dfrac{K(r+R)}{(R^2+r^2)}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assume that an electric field $\vec{E} = 30x^2\,\hat{i}$ N/C exists in space. If $V_0$ is the potential at the origin and $V_A$ is the potential at $x = 2$ m, then the potential difference $V_A - V_0$ is
questions.solution:
The relation between electric potential and electric field is given by $dV = -\vec{E} \cdot d\vec{r}$.

Since the electric field is only in the x-direction, we have $dV = -E_x dx$.

Integrating both sides from the origin ($x = 0$) to $x = 2$ m:

$\int_{V_0}^{V_A} dV = -\int_{0}^{2} 30x^2 dx$

$V_A - V_0 = -30 \left[ \dfrac{x^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{2}$

$V_A - V_0 = -10 (2^3 - 0^3)$

$V_A - V_0 = -80$ V.

Answer: $-80\ V$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Equipotential surfaces associated with an electric field that is increasing in magnitude along the positive Y-direction are
questions.solution:
The electric field is directed along the Y-axis.

Equipotential surfaces are always perpendicular to the direction of the electric field.

Since the electric field is along the Y-direction, the equipotential surfaces must be perpendicular to the Y-axis.

Planes perpendicular to the Y-axis are parallel to the XZ-plane.

The increasing magnitude of the electric field along the positive Y-direction implies that the spacing between consecutive equipotential surfaces decreases along this direction, but their orientation remains parallel to the XZ-plane.

Answer: planes parallel to XZ-plane.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge $Q$ is divided into two parts and placed at a given distance so that the force between them is maximum. The value of each charge is:
questions.solution:
Let the two parts of the charge $Q$ be $q$ and $Q - q$.

The electrostatic force between them when placed at a given distance $r$ is given by Coulomb's law:

$F = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \dfrac{q(Q - q)}{r^2}$

For the force to be maximum, the first derivative of $F$ with respect to $q$ must be zero:

$\dfrac{dF}{dq} = 0$

$\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2} \dfrac{d}{dq}(qQ - q^2) = 0$

Since $\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}$ is a non-zero constant, we get:

$\dfrac{d}{dq}(qQ - q^2) = 0$

$Q - 2q = 0$

$2q = Q$

$q = \dfrac{Q}{2}$

The other part of the charge is $Q - q = Q - \dfrac{Q}{2} = \dfrac{Q}{2}$.

Thus, the value of each charge is $\dfrac{Q}{2}$ and $\dfrac{Q}{2}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two point charges $+3$ C and $+6$ C separated by a distance 'r' , repel each other with a force of 27 N. If each of the above charges is given an additional charge of $-5$ C, the force experienced by them is
questions.solution:
Initial charges are $q_1 = +3\text{ C}$ and $q_2 = +6\text{ C}$.

Initial force $F_1 = \dfrac{k q_1 q_2}{r^2} = \dfrac{k (3)(6)}{r^2} = \dfrac{18k}{r^2} = 27\text{ N}$.

This gives $\dfrac{k}{r^2} = \dfrac{27}{18} = 1.5\text{ N}$.

When an additional charge of $-5\text{ C}$ is given to each, the new charges become:

$q_1' = +3 - 5 = -2\text{ C}$

$q_2' = +6 - 5 = +1\text{ C}$

The new force $F_2 = \dfrac{k |q_1'| |q_2'|}{r^2} = \dfrac{k (2)(1)}{r^2} = \dfrac{2k}{r^2}$.

Substituting the value of $\dfrac{k}{r^2}$, we get:

$F_2 = 2 \times 1.5 = 3\text{ N}$.

Since the new charges have opposite signs, the force between them is attractive.

Answer: $3\text{N}$ (attractive)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
An electric dipole is placed parallel to the electric field . Let 'W' be the work done in rotating the dipole by $60^\circ$ . Now, it is further rotated by $120^\circ$, the total work done is
$[\sin 30^\circ = \cos 60^\circ = 0.5,\ \cos 0^\circ = \sin 90^\circ = 1,\ \cos\pi^c = -1]$
questions.solution:
The work done in rotating an electric dipole in a uniform electric field from an initial angle $\theta_1$ to a final angle $\theta_2$ is given by $W = pE(\cos\theta_1 - \cos\theta_2)$.

Initially, the dipole is parallel to the electric field, so $\theta_1 = 0^\circ$.

The work done in rotating the dipole by $60^\circ$ is:

$W = pE(\cos 0^\circ - \cos 60^\circ)$

$W = pE(1 - 0.5) = 0.5 pE$

$\Rightarrow pE = 2W$

The dipole is further rotated by $120^\circ$, making the final angle with the electric field $\theta_2 = 60^\circ + 120^\circ = 180^\circ$.

The total work done from the initial position is:

$W_{\text{total}} = pE(\cos 0^\circ - \cos 180^\circ)$

$W_{\text{total}} = pE(1 - (-1)) = 2pE$

Substituting $pE = 2W$:

$W_{\text{total}} = 2(2W) = 4W$

Answer: $4\text{W}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The potential at a point x ($\mu$m) due to some charges situated on the X - axis is given by
$V(x) = x^2 + 1.5 x + 2$ volt
The electric field E at $x = 3\ \mu$m is given by
questions.solution:
The electric potential is given by $V(x) = x^2 + 1.5 x + 2$

The electric field is related to the potential by the equation $E = -\dfrac{dV}{dx}$

Differentiating the given potential with respect to $x$:

$E = -\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^2 + 1.5 x + 2) = -(2x + 1.5)$

Substituting $x = 3\ \mu\text{m}$:

$E = -(2(3) + 1.5) = -7.5\ \text{V}/\mu\text{m}$

The magnitude of the electric field is $7.5\ \text{V}/\mu\text{m}$ and the negative sign indicates that it is directed in the negative X-direction.

Answer: $7.5\ \text{V}/\mu\text{m}$ and in the -ve X - direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Four point charges $-Q$, $-q$, $2q$ and $3Q$ are placed at corners of the square respectively. The relation between $Q$ and $q$ for which the potential at the centre of the square is zero, is
questions.solution:
Let the side length of the square be $a$. The distance of the centre from each corner is $r = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{2}}$.

The electric potential at the centre of the square is the algebraic sum of the potentials due to all four charges.

$V = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \left( \dfrac{-Q}{r} + \dfrac{-q}{r} + \dfrac{2q}{r} + \dfrac{3Q}{r} \right)$

Given that $V = 0$, we have:

$-Q - q + 2q + 3Q = 0$

$2Q + q = 0$

$Q = -\dfrac{q}{2}$

Answer: $Q = -q/2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two charges $q_1$ and $q_2$ are separated with distance 'd' and placed in a medium of dielectric constant 'k'. The equivalent distance between charges in air for the same electrostatic force is
questions.solution:
Force between two charges in a medium of dielectric constant $k$ is given by

$F_{m} = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_{0} k} \dfrac{q_{1} q_{2}}{d^{2}}$

Force between the same charges in air at an equivalent distance $d'$ is given by

$F_{a} = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_{0}} \dfrac{q_{1} q_{2}}{d'^{2}}$

Given that the electrostatic force remains the same, $F_{m} = F_{a}$

$\dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_{0} k} \dfrac{q_{1} q_{2}}{d^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_{0}} \dfrac{q_{1} q_{2}}{d'^{2}}$

$\dfrac{1}{k d^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{d'^{2}}$

$d'^{2} = k d^{2}$

$d' = d\sqrt{k}$

Answer: $d\sqrt{k}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The electric potential due to a dipole at point P making an angle $120^\circ$ with the axis of a dipole is
($\varepsilon_0$ = permitivity of free space, $p$ = electric dipole moment , $r$ = distance of point P from the center of dipole)
($\cos 60^\circ = 1/2$)
questions.solution:
The electric potential $V$ due to a dipole at a distance $r$ and angle $\theta$ from the dipole axis is given by

$V = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \dfrac{p \cos\theta}{r^2}$

Given $\theta = 120^\circ$, we have $\cos 120^\circ = -\cos 60^\circ = -\dfrac{1}{2}$

Substituting this value into the formula, we get

$V = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \dfrac{p \left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\right)}{r^2}$

$V = \dfrac{-1}{8\pi\varepsilon_0} \dfrac{p}{r^2}$

Answer: $\dfrac{-1}{8\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{p}{r^2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The electric potential ' $V$ ' at a radial distance $x$ just outside a non-conducting sphere is $\dfrac{\rho R^2}{2 \varepsilon_0 x}$. The electric intensity ' E ' at a radial distance $x$ is ( $\rho$ is uniform volume charge density, $R$ is the radius of the sphere and $\varepsilon_0$ is the permittivity of free space)
questions.solution:
The relation between electric field intensity $E$ and electric potential $V$ in a radial field is given by $E = -\dfrac{dV}{dx}$.

Given the electric potential $V = \dfrac{\rho R^2}{2\varepsilon_0 x}$.

Differentiating $V$ with respect to $x$ to find $E$:

$E = -\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\dfrac{\rho R^2}{2\varepsilon_0 x}\right)$

$E = -\dfrac{\rho R^2}{2\varepsilon_0}\left(-\dfrac{1}{x^2}\right)$

$E = \dfrac{\rho R^2}{2\varepsilon_0 x^2}$ Answer: $\dfrac{\rho R^2}{2\epsilon_0 x}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Three point charges $+4e$ , $+2e$ and $+e$ are kept at each vertex of an equilateral triangle ABC , each of side $a$ . The net electric intensity acting on charge $+e$ is
( $\cos 60^\circ = 1/2$ and $k$ is constant )
questions.solution:
Let the charge $+e$ be placed at vertex C of the equilateral triangle ABC, while $+4e$ and $+2e$ are placed at vertices A and B respectively.

The electric intensity (electric field) at C due to the charge $+4e$ at A is:
$E_1 = \dfrac{k(4e)}{a^2}$

The electric intensity at C due to the charge $+2e$ at B is:
$E_2 = \dfrac{k(2e)}{a^2}$

Since ABC is an equilateral triangle, the angle between the electric field vectors $\vec{E}_1$ and $\vec{E}_2$ is $60^\circ$.

The net electric intensity at C is given by the vector sum of $\vec{E}_1$ and $\vec{E}_2$:
$E_{net} = \sqrt{E_1^2 + E_2^2 + 2E_1 E_2 \cos 60^\circ}$

Substituting the values of $E_1$, $E_2$, and $\cos 60^\circ = \dfrac{1}{2}$:
$E_{net} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{4ke}{a^2}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{2ke}{a^2}\right)^2 + 2\left(\dfrac{4ke}{a^2}\right)\left(\dfrac{2ke}{a^2}\right)\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)}$

$E_{net} = \dfrac{ke}{a^2} \sqrt{16 + 4 + 8}$

$E_{net} = \dfrac{ke}{a^2} \sqrt{28}$

$E_{net} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{7}\,ke}{a^2}$

Answer: $\dfrac{2\sqrt{7}\,ke}{a^2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Consider a thin spherical shell of radius R with its centre at the origin, carrying uniform positive surface charge density. The variation of the magnitude of the electric field $E(r)$ and electric potential $V(r)$ with the distance r from the centre, is best represented by which graph?

Continuous curve stands for intensity. Dotted curve stands for potential.
questions.solution:
For a thin spherical shell of radius $R$ with a uniform positive surface charge density, the electric field $E(r)$ and electric potential $V(r)$ are given by:

1. Electric Field ($E$):
Inside the shell ($r < R$), the electric field is zero because the charge enclosed by any Gaussian surface inside the shell is zero.
$E(r) = 0$ for $r < R$
Outside the shell ($r \ge R$), the shell behaves as a point charge located at its centre.
$E(r) = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \dfrac{Q}{r^2}$ for $r \ge R$
Thus, the continuous curve representing $|E(r)|$ should be zero for $r < R$, jump to a maximum at $r = R$, and then decrease as $\dfrac{1}{r^2}$.

2. Electric Potential ($V$):
Inside the shell ($r \le R$), the electric field is zero, so the potential is constant and equal to the potential at the surface.
$V(r) = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \dfrac{Q}{R}$ for $r \le R$
Outside the shell ($r > R$), the potential decreases inversely with distance.
$V(r) = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \dfrac{Q}{r}$ for $r > R$
Thus, the dotted curve representing $V(r)$ should be a horizontal line (constant) for $r \le R$ and then decrease as $\dfrac{1}{r}$.

Graph D is the only one that correctly shows the continuous curve as zero for $r < R$ and the dotted curve as constant for $r \le R$.

Answer: D

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The positively charged particle is released from the rest in a uniform electric field. The electric potential energy of the charge
a. decreases because the charge moves along the electric field.
b. decreases because the charge moves opposite the electric field.
c. remains constant because the electric field is uniform.
d. increases because the charge moves along the electric field.
questions.solution:
When a positively charged particle is released from rest in a uniform electric field, it experiences an electric force $\vec{F} = q\vec{E}$ in the direction of the electric field.

Thus, the particle moves along the direction of the electric field.

The work done by the electric field on the positive charge is positive. Since the change in electric potential energy is the negative of the work done by the conservative electric force ($\Delta U = -W$), the electric potential energy of the charge decreases.

Alternatively, electric potential $V$ decreases in the direction of the electric field. Since $U = qV$ and $q$ is positive, the electric potential energy decreases as the charge moves along the electric field.

Answer: a

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of mass '$M$' and charge '$q$' initially at rest is accelerated by a uniform electric field '$E$' through a distance '$D$' and is then allowed to approach a fixed static charge '$Q$' of the same sign. The distance of the closest approach of the charge $q$ is $[\,\epsilon_0$ = permittivity of free space $]$
questions.solution:
Work done by the uniform electric field on the particle is given by $W = qED$.

By the work-energy theorem, the kinetic energy gained by the particle is $K = qED$.

When the particle approaches the fixed static charge $Q$, its kinetic energy is converted into electrostatic potential energy.

At the distance of closest approach $r$, the velocity of the particle becomes zero and the entire kinetic energy is stored as potential energy.

The electrostatic potential energy at distance $r$ is $U = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \dfrac{qQ}{r}$.

Equating the kinetic energy and potential energy:

$qED = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \dfrac{qQ}{r}$

$r = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\epsilon_0 ED}$

Answer: $Q/4\pi\epsilon_0 ED$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Pressure exerted by an ideal gas on the walls of the container is equal to
a) mean kinetic energy per unit volume.
b) half of mean kinetic energy per unit volume.
c) one-third of mean kinetic energy per unit volume.
d) two-third of mean kinetic energy per unit volume.
questions.solution:
The pressure exerted by an ideal gas is given by $P = \dfrac{1}{3} \rho v_{rms}^2$, where $\rho$ is the density of the gas and $v_{rms}$ is the root mean square velocity.

The mean kinetic energy per unit volume is given by $E = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v_{rms}^2$.

From these two equations, we can write $P = \dfrac{2}{3} \left( \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v_{rms}^2 \right) = \dfrac{2}{3} E$.

Therefore, the pressure exerted by an ideal gas is equal to two-thirds of the mean kinetic energy per unit volume.

Answer: d

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two litre of an ideal gas at $42^\circ\text{C}$ is heated at a constant pressure to $357^\circ\text{C}$. Then the final volume is
questions.solution:
Given, initial volume $V_1 = 2\text{L}$

Initial temperature $T_1 = 42^\circ\text{C} = 42 + 273 = 315\text{K}$

Final temperature $T_2 = 357^\circ\text{C} = 357 + 273 = 630\text{K}$

At constant pressure, using Charles's law:

$\dfrac{V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{V_2}{T_2}$

$\dfrac{2}{315} = \dfrac{V_2}{630}$

$V_2 = \dfrac{2 \times 630}{315} = 4\text{L}$

Answer: $4\text{L}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The temperature at which the mean kinetic energy of the molecules of gas is $\left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right)^{th}$ of the mean kinetic energy of its molecules at $147^\circ\text{C}$ is
questions.solution:
The mean kinetic energy of gas molecules is directly proportional to the absolute temperature of the gas, $E \propto T$.

Let the initial temperature be $T_1 = 147^{\circ}\text{C} = 147 + 273 = 420\text{ K}$.

Let the final temperature be $T_2$.

Given that $E_2 = \dfrac{1}{4}E_1$.

Therefore, $T_2 = \dfrac{1}{4}T_1 = \dfrac{1}{4} \times 420\text{ K} = 105\text{ K}$.

Answer: $105$ K

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: There are two vessels A and B with rigid walls containing ideal gases. The pressure, temperature and volume are $P_A$, $T_A$ and V in the vessel A and $P_B$, $T_B$ and V in vessel B. The vessels are now connected through a small tube. In equilibrium, pressure is P and temperature is T, then $\dfrac{P}{T}$ is {number of moles of gas in vessel A $=$ number of moles of gas in vessel B}
questions.solution:
Using the ideal gas equation, the initial number of moles in vessel A is:

$n_A = \dfrac{P_A V}{R T_A}$

The initial number of moles in vessel B is:

$n_B = \dfrac{P_B V}{R T_B}$

When the vessels are connected, the total volume becomes $V_{total} = V + V = 2V$. Let the equilibrium pressure be $P$ and the equilibrium temperature be $T$. The total number of moles in the system remains conserved:

$n_{total} = n_A + n_B$

$\dfrac{P(2V)}{RT} = \dfrac{P_A V}{RT_A} + \dfrac{P_B V}{RT_B}$

Dividing the entire equation by $\dfrac{V}{R}$:

$\dfrac{2P}{T} = \dfrac{P_A}{T_A} + \dfrac{P_B}{T_B}$

$\dfrac{P}{T} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{P_A}{T_A} + \dfrac{P_B}{T_B}\right)$

Answer: $\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{P_A}{T_A}+\dfrac{P_B}{T_B}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The pressure $P$, volume $V$ and temperature $T$ of a gas in the jar A and the other gas in the jar B is at pressure $2P$, volume $V/4$ and temperature $2T$, then the ratio of the number of molecules in the jar A and B will be
questions.solution:
Using the ideal gas equation, $PV = NkT$, where $N$ is the number of molecules.

For jar A:
$N_A = \dfrac{PV}{kT}$

For jar B:
$N_B = \dfrac{P_B V_B}{k T_B} = \dfrac{(2P)(V/4)}{k(2T)} = \dfrac{PV/2}{2kT} = \dfrac{PV}{4kT}$

Ratio of number of molecules:
$\dfrac{N_A}{N_B} = \dfrac{\dfrac{PV}{kT}}{\dfrac{PV}{4kT}} = \dfrac{4}{1}$

The ratio is $4 : 1$.

Answer: $4 : 1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The temperature at which the root-mean-square speed of carbon dioxide molecules becomes just sufficient for escaping Earth's atmosphere is approximately
(Mass of carbon dioxide molecule $= 7 \times 10^{-26}\ \text{kg}$, Boltzmann's constant $k_B = 14 \times 10^{-24}\ J/K$, escape velocity from Earth's surface $= 11\ \text{km/s}$)
questions.solution:
The root-mean-square speed of gas molecules is given by:

$v_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 k_B T}{m}}$

For the molecules to escape Earth's atmosphere, their rms speed must be equal to the escape velocity $v_e$:

$\sqrt{\dfrac{3 k_B T}{m}} = v_e$

Squaring both sides and solving for $T$:

$T = \dfrac{m v_e^2}{3 k_B}$

Substituting the given values ($m = 7 \times 10^{-26}\ \text{kg}$, $v_e = 11 \times 10^3\ \text{m/s}$, $k_B = 14 \times 10^{-24}\ \text{J/K}$):

$T = \dfrac{7 \times 10^{-26} \times (11 \times 10^3)^2}{3 \times 14 \times 10^{-24}}$

$T = \dfrac{7 \times 121 \times 10^{-20}}{42 \times 10^{-24}}$

$T = \dfrac{121}{6} \times 10^4$

$T \approx 20.166 \times 10^4\ \text{K}$

$T \approx 2.016 \times 10^5\ \text{K}$

Answer: $2.016 \times 10^5\ \text{K}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the ratio of specific heat of gas $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v}$, then the value of $C_p$ in terms of $\gamma$ and gas constant $R$ is
questions.solution:
Given $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v}$

Using Mayer's relation, $C_p - C_v = R$

Substituting $C_v = \dfrac{C_p}{\gamma}$ into the relation:

$C_p - \dfrac{C_p}{\gamma} = R$

$C_p \left( \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} \right) = R$

$C_p = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$

Answer: $\dfrac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The internal energy per molecule for a diatomic (non-rigid) gas is (where $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant and $T$ is absolute temperature).
questions.solution:
For a non-rigid diatomic gas, the molecules possess translational, rotational, and vibrational motion.

The degrees of freedom are distributed as follows:
Translational degrees of freedom = $3$
Rotational degrees of freedom = $2$
Vibrational degrees of freedom = $2$ (one for kinetic energy and one for potential energy)

Total degrees of freedom $f = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7$

According to the law of equipartition of energy, the average energy associated with each degree of freedom per molecule is $\dfrac{1}{2}k_B T$.

Internal energy per molecule = $\dfrac{f}{2} k_B T = \dfrac{7}{2} k_B T$

Answer: $\dfrac{7}{2}k_B T$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: According to law of equi-partition of energy the molar specific heat of a rigid diatomic gas at constant pressure is
questions.solution:
For a rigid diatomic gas, the number of translational degrees of freedom is $3$ and the number of rotational degrees of freedom is $2$.

Total degrees of freedom, $f = 3 + 2 = 5$.

According to the law of equipartition of energy, the molar specific heat at constant volume is given by:

$C_v = \dfrac{f}{2} R = \dfrac{5}{2} R$

Using Mayer's relation, the molar specific heat at constant pressure is:

$C_p = C_v + R$

$C_p = \dfrac{5}{2} R + R = \dfrac{7}{2} R$

Answer: $\dfrac{7R}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Statement I- The kinetic energy per molecule of the gas is equal to $\dfrac{3}{2}k_B T$.
$\{k_B$ = Boltzmann constant, T = absolute temperature of the gas$\}$
Statement II- For the ideal gas with total energy E, the product $PV=\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right)E$
$\{$P = pressure of the gas and V = volume of the gas$\}$
options: Statement I is incorrect and statement II is correct.
Statement I is correct and statement II is incorrect.
questions.solution:
The average translational kinetic energy per molecule of an ideal gas is given by $\dfrac{3}{2}k_B T$. Thus, Statement I is correct.

The total translational kinetic energy of an ideal gas is $E = \dfrac{3}{2}PV$. Rearranging this relation gives $PV = \dfrac{2}{3}E$. Thus, Statement II is incorrect.

Therefore, Statement I is correct and Statement II is incorrect.

Answer: Statement I is correct and statement II is incorrect.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The total internal energy of one mole of HCl molecule is (assume the molecule to be non-rigid)
questions.solution:
HCl is a diatomic molecule.

For a non-rigid diatomic molecule, the degrees of freedom are:
Translational degrees of freedom = $3$
Rotational degrees of freedom = $2$
Vibrational degrees of freedom = $2$ (since each vibrational mode contributes two degrees of freedom, one for kinetic energy and one for potential energy).

Total degrees of freedom $f = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7$.

By the law of equipartition of energy, the internal energy of one mole of a gas is given by $U = \dfrac{f}{2}RT$.

Substituting $f = 7$, we get $U = \dfrac{7}{2}RT$.

Answer: $\dfrac{7}{2}RT$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the pressure of the gas is kept constant, then the mean free path ($\lambda$) of a gas varies with temperature ($T$) according to relation
questions.solution:
The mean free path of a gas molecule is given by

$\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}\pi d^2 n}$

where $d$ is the diameter of the molecule and $n$ is the number of molecules per unit volume.

From the ideal gas equation, $P = n k_B T$, which gives $n = \dfrac{P}{k_B T}$.

Substituting this into the expression for mean free path, we get

$\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\pi d^2 P}$

For a given gas at constant pressure, $P$ is constant. Therefore,

$\lambda \propto T$

Answer: $\lambda \propto T$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The Translational K.E. of the molecules of a gas at absolute temperature (T) can be doubled by
questions.solution:
The average translational kinetic energy of a gas molecule is given by $E = \dfrac{3}{2} k_B T$.

This shows that the translational kinetic energy is directly proportional to the absolute temperature of the gas, i.e., $E \propto T$.

To double the translational kinetic energy, the absolute temperature must be doubled.

Therefore, the temperature should be increased to $2T$.

Answer: increasing T to 2 T

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two identical vessels are completely filled with two ideal gases A and B. If the ratio of mass of gas A to that of gas B is 1:2 and the ratio of rms speed of molecules of gas A to that of gas B is 2:1, the ratio of pressure exerted by molecules of gas B, on the walls of vessel to that by molecules of gas A is
questions.solution:
The pressure exerted by an ideal gas is given by $P = \dfrac{1}{3} \rho v_{rms}^2 = \dfrac{1}{3} \dfrac{m}{V} v_{rms}^2$

Since the vessels are identical, the volume $V$ is the same for both gases.

Therefore, $P \propto m v_{rms}^2$

The ratio of pressures is given by:

$\dfrac{P_B}{P_A} = \dfrac{m_B}{m_A} \times \left( \dfrac{v_{rms, B}}{v_{rms, A}} \right)^2$

Given $\dfrac{m_A}{m_B} = \dfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \dfrac{m_B}{m_A} = 2$

And $\dfrac{v_{rms, A}}{v_{rms, B}} = \dfrac{2}{1} \Rightarrow \dfrac{v_{rms, B}}{v_{rms, A}} = \dfrac{1}{2}$

Substituting the values:

$\dfrac{P_B}{P_A} = 2 \times \left( \dfrac{1}{2} \right)^2 = 2 \times \dfrac{1}{4} = \dfrac{1}{2}$

The ratio of pressure exerted by gas B to that by gas A is $1 : 2$.

Answer: $1 : 2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a rigid diatomic molecule, universal gas constant $R = n C_p$ where '$C_p$' is the molar specific heat at constant pressure. The value of fractional number '$n$' is
questions.solution:
For a rigid diatomic molecule, the number of degrees of freedom is $f = 5$.

The molar specific heat at constant volume is $C_v = \dfrac{f}{2} R = \dfrac{5}{2} R$.

The molar specific heat at constant pressure is $C_p = C_v + R = \dfrac{5}{2} R + R = \dfrac{7}{2} R$.

Given the relation $R = n C_p$.

Substituting the value of $C_p$, we get $R = n \left( \dfrac{7}{2} R \right)$.

Solving for $n$, we get $n = \dfrac{2}{7}$.

Answer: $2/7$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If a non-linear polyatomic gas has $f$ vibrational nodes, the value of $\gamma = C_p/C_v$ is ($C_p$ and $C_v$ are specific heats at constant pressure and volume respectively)
questions.solution:
For a non-linear polyatomic gas, the number of translational degrees of freedom is $3$ and the number of rotational degrees of freedom is $3$.

Each vibrational mode contributes $2$ degrees of freedom, one associated with kinetic energy and the other with potential energy. Therefore, $f$ vibrational modes contribute $2f$ degrees of freedom.

The total number of degrees of freedom $N$ is:

$N = 3 + 3 + 2f = 6 + 2f$

The molar specific heat at constant volume $C_v$ is given by $\dfrac{N}{2}R$.

$C_v = \dfrac{6 + 2f}{2}R = (3 + f)R$

Using Mayer's relation, the molar specific heat at constant pressure $C_p$ is:

$C_p = C_v + R$

$C_p = (3 + f)R + R = (4 + f)R$

The ratio of specific heats $\gamma$ is given by $\dfrac{C_p}{C_v}$.

$\gamma = \dfrac{(4 + f)R}{(3 + f)R}$

$\gamma = \dfrac{4 + f}{3 + f}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A vessel is filled with a gas at a pressure of 76 cm of mercury at a certain temperature. The mass of the gas is increased by 75 % by introducing more gas in the vessel at the same temperature. The resultant pressure of the gas is
questions.solution:
From the ideal gas equation, $PV = nRT = \dfrac{m}{M}RT$.

Since the volume $V$ of the vessel and the temperature $T$ remain constant, the pressure of the gas is directly proportional to its mass $m$.

$P \propto m$

$\Rightarrow \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{m_2}{m_1}$

Given that the mass of the gas is increased by $75\%$, the final mass is:

$m_2 = m_1 + 0.75m_1 = 1.75m_1$

Substituting the values into the pressure relation:

$\dfrac{P_2}{76} = \dfrac{1.75m_1}{m_1}$

$P_2 = 1.75 \times 76 = 133$ cm of Hg.

Answer: 133 cm of mercury

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $C_p$ and $C_v$ are the specific heats at constant pressure and constant volume respectively. It is observed that $C_p - C_v = a$ for hydrogen gas and $C_p - C_v = b$ for carbon dioxide gas. The correct relationship between ' a' and 'b' is ( atomic weight of hydrogen , carbon and oxygen are 1u, 12 u and 16 u respectively)
questions.solution:
For a given gas, the molar heat capacities at constant pressure and constant volume are related by Mayer's relation:

$C_{P,m} - C_{V,m} = R$

The specific heat is defined as the heat capacity per unit mass. Therefore, the difference between the specific heats at constant pressure ($C_p$) and constant volume ($C_v$) is given by:

$C_p - C_v = \dfrac{R}{M}$

where $M$ is the molar mass of the gas.

For hydrogen gas ($H_2$), the molar mass is $M_{H_2} = 2 \times 1 = 2$ u. Thus:

$a = \dfrac{R}{2}$

For carbon dioxide gas ($CO_2$), the molar mass is $M_{CO_2} = 12 + 2 \times 16 = 44$ u. Thus:

$b = \dfrac{R}{44}$

Taking the ratio of $a$ to $b$:

$\dfrac{a}{b} = \dfrac{\dfrac{R}{2}}{\dfrac{R}{44}} = \dfrac{44}{2} = 22$

$\Rightarrow a = 22 b$

Answer: $a = 22\ b$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnitude of the magnetic induction (B) at a point on the axis at a large distance 'r' from the centre of a circular coil of 'n' turns and area 'A' carrying current ' i ' is given by $x \times 10^{-7}$ SI unit. The value of x is $(\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ SI unit$)$
questions.solution:
The magnetic field on the axis of a circular coil of radius $R$ at a distance $r$ from its centre is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 n i R^2}{2(R^2 + r^2)^{3/2}}$

For a large distance $r \gg R$, the expression simplifies to:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 n i R^2}{2r^3}$

Multiplying the numerator and denominator by $\pi$, we get:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 n i (\pi R^2)}{2\pi r^3}$

Since the area of the coil is $A = \pi R^2$, we can write:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 n i A}{2\pi r^3}$

Substituting $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ SI units:

$B = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times n i A}{2\pi r^3} = \dfrac{2 n i A}{r^3} \times 10^{-7}$

Comparing this with $B = x \times 10^{-7}$, we find:

$x = \dfrac{2 n i A}{r^3}$

Answer: $\dfrac{2niA}{r^3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a circular coil of radius $a$, at what distance $x$ along its axis the magnetic field is exactly $\dfrac{1}{27}$ of its value at the center?
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the center of a circular coil of radius $a$ is given by $B_c = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2a}$.

The magnetic field at a distance $x$ along the axis is given by $B_x = \dfrac{\mu_0 I a^2}{2(a^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$.

Given that $B_x = \dfrac{1}{27} B_c$, we have:

$\dfrac{\mu_0 I a^2}{2(a^2 + x^2)^{3/2}} = \dfrac{1}{27} \left( \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2a} \right)$

$\dfrac{a^3}{(a^2 + x^2)^{3/2}} = \dfrac{1}{27}$

Taking the cube root on both sides:

$\dfrac{a}{\sqrt{a^2 + x^2}} = \dfrac{1}{3}$

Squaring both sides:

$\dfrac{a^2}{a^2 + x^2} = \dfrac{1}{9}$

$9a^2 = a^2 + x^2$

$x^2 = 8a^2$

$x = 2\sqrt{2}a$

Answer: $2\sqrt{2}a$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circular coil having 'n' turns, each of radius 'r' carries a current 'I'. The magnetic field at a distance $x = \sqrt{8}\ r$ from its centre along axis of the coil is $(\mu_0 = $ permeability of free space)
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at a distance $x$ on the axis of a circular coil of $n$ turns, radius $r$, carrying current $I$ is given by

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 n I r^2}{2(r^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$

Given $x = \sqrt{8} r$, substituting this value in the formula:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 n I r^2}{2(r^2 + (\sqrt{8} r)^2)^{3/2}}$

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 n I r^2}{2(r^2 + 8r^2)^{3/2}}$

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 n I r^2}{2(9r^2)^{3/2}}$

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 n I r^2}{2(27r^3)}$

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 n I}{54 r}$

Answer: $\dfrac{\mu_0 nI}{54\ r}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of cyclotron frequency $f$ to the radius of the path of a charged particle $R$ is $\dfrac{q^2 B^2}{2\pi m^2}x$. Then the value of $x$ is ( $v$ is velocity of charged particle)
questions.solution:
The radius of the circular path of a charged particle in a uniform magnetic field is given by $R = \dfrac{mv}{qB}$.

The cyclotron frequency is given by $f = \dfrac{qB}{2\pi m}$.

Taking the ratio of the cyclotron frequency to the radius, we get:

$\dfrac{f}{R} = \dfrac{\dfrac{qB}{2\pi m}}{\dfrac{mv}{qB}} = \dfrac{q^2 B^2}{2\pi m^2 v}$

It is given that $\dfrac{f}{R} = \dfrac{q^2 B^2}{2\pi m^2}x$.

Comparing the two expressions, we get $x = \dfrac{1}{v} = v^{-1}$.

Answer: $v^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a cyclotron, for the same values of B and R, what is the ratio of the maximum kinetic energies of $\alpha$ particles and deuteron? {Mass of deuteron $=2m_p$, charge on deuteron $=+e$}
questions.solution:
The maximum kinetic energy of a particle in a cyclotron is given by $K = \dfrac{q^2 B^2 R^2}{2m}$.

For an $\alpha$ particle, charge $q_{\alpha} = 2e$ and mass $m_{\alpha} = 4m_p$.

$K_{\alpha} = \dfrac{(2e)^2 B^2 R^2}{2(4m_p)} = \dfrac{4e^2 B^2 R^2}{8m_p} = \dfrac{e^2 B^2 R^2}{2m_p}$

For a deuteron, charge $q_d = e$ and mass $m_d = 2m_p$.

$K_d = \dfrac{e^2 B^2 R^2}{2(2m_p)} = \dfrac{e^2 B^2 R^2}{4m_p}$

The ratio of their maximum kinetic energies is:

$\dfrac{K_{\alpha}}{K_d} = \dfrac{\dfrac{e^2 B^2 R^2}{2m_p}}{\dfrac{e^2 B^2 R^2}{4m_p}} = \dfrac{4}{2} = 2$

Answer: $2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The magnitude of the magnetic field at point O (centre of the arc) with radius R, in the following case is
($\mu_0$ = permeability of free space, I = current and R is the radius of circular arc)
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the center O is the sum of the magnetic fields due to the straight wire segments and the circular arc.

The straight wire segments lie along the radial lines passing through the center O. For these segments, the angle between the current element $d\vec{l}$ and the position vector $\vec{r}$ is $0^\circ$ or $180^\circ$. Thus, $d\vec{l} \times \vec{r} = 0$, and their contribution to the magnetic field at O is zero.

The circular arc subtends an angle $\theta = 360^\circ - 90^\circ = 270^\circ = \dfrac{3\pi}{2}$ radians at the center.

The magnetic field due to a circular arc at its center is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi R} \theta$

Substituting $\theta = \dfrac{3\pi}{2}$:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi R} \left(\dfrac{3\pi}{2}\right) = \dfrac{3\mu_0 I}{8R}$

Answer: $\dfrac{3\mu_0 I}{8R}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two wires of same material A and B, having lengths $l$ and $\dfrac{2l}{3}$ carry the currents $I$ and $\dfrac{3I}{4}$ respectively. Both the wires (A and B) are bent into a circles (of single turn). If $B_A$ and $B_B$ are the values of magnetic field at the centres of the respective circles, then the ratio $\dfrac{B_B}{B_A}$ is
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of a circular loop of radius $R$ carrying current $I$ is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2R}$

Since the wire of length $L$ is bent into a single turn circular loop, the circumference is equal to the length of the wire:

$2\pi R = L \Rightarrow R = \dfrac{L}{2\pi}$

Substituting the value of $R$ in the expression for $B$:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 \left(\dfrac{L}{2\pi}\right)} = \dfrac{\mu_0 \pi I}{L}$

For wire A:

$L_A = l$ and $I_A = I$

$B_A = \dfrac{\mu_0 \pi I}{l}$

For wire B:

$L_B = \dfrac{2l}{3}$ and $I_B = \dfrac{3I}{4}$

$B_B = \dfrac{\mu_0 \pi \left(\dfrac{3I}{4}\right)}{\dfrac{2l}{3}} = \dfrac{9 \mu_0 \pi I}{8l}$

Taking the ratio of the magnetic fields:

$\dfrac{B_B}{B_A} = \dfrac{\dfrac{9 \mu_0 \pi I}{8l}}{\dfrac{\mu_0 \pi I}{l}} = \dfrac{9}{8}$

Answer: $\dfrac{9}{8}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
An electron is projected with uniform velocity along the axis of a current carrying long solenoid. Which of the following is true?
a. The electron will be accelerated along the axis.
b. The electron path will be circular about the axis.
c. The electron will experience a force at $45^\circ$ to the axis and hence execute a helical path.
d. The electron will continue to move with uniform velocity along the axis of the solenoid.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field inside a long current-carrying solenoid is uniform and directed along its axis.

The electron is projected with a uniform velocity along the axis of the solenoid, which means its velocity vector $\vec{v}$ is parallel or anti-parallel to the magnetic field vector $\vec{B}$.

The magnetic force experienced by a moving charge is given by $\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$.

Since the angle $\theta$ between $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$ is either $0^\circ$ or $180^\circ$, the cross product $\vec{v} \times \vec{B} = vB\sin\theta = 0$.

Therefore, the magnetic force acting on the electron is zero. In the absence of any force, the electron will not accelerate and will continue to move with its initial uniform velocity along the axis of the solenoid.

Answer: d

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two concentric coils, each of radius equal to $2\pi$ cm are placed at right angle to each other. If $3$ A and $4$ A are the currents flowing through the two coils, respectively. The magnetic induction (in Wb/m$^2$) at the centre of the coils will be {$\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ SI unit}
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of a circular coil is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2R}$.

For the first coil, $B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_1}{2R}$.

For the second coil, $B_2 = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_2}{2R}$.

Since the coils are placed at right angles to each other, their magnetic fields at the centre are perpendicular. The resultant magnetic field is:

$B = \sqrt{B_1^2 + B_2^2} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{2R} \sqrt{I_1^2 + I_2^2}$

Substituting the given values $R = 2\pi \times 10^{-2}$ m, $I_1 = 3$ A, $I_2 = 4$ A, and $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ T m/A:

$B = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7}}{2 \times 2\pi \times 10^{-2}} \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2}$

$B = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7}}{4\pi \times 10^{-2}} \times 5$
